proposals.title: UniStream: Emergent Multimodal Alignment from a Single Next-Token Objective on Unpaired Image and Text Streams
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal models (e.g., CLIP, Flamingo) rely on curated, aligned image-text pairs, a costly and potentially limiting bottleneck for scaling. This work challenges the necessity of this alignment, asking if a single, unified model can learn meaningful cross-modal representations from completely unpaired, large-scale streams of images and text, mirroring how humans learn from separate sensory inputs.
proposals.hypothesis: A transformer trained with a single next-token prediction objective on a concatenated, temporally interleaved but semantically unaligned stream of image patches and text tokens will develop emergent, semantically meaningful alignment between the modalities without any paired supervision.
proposals.method: We will train a decoder-only transformer (e.g., a modified PaLM architecture) on a massive, interleaved stream of image patches (via VQ-VAE or ViT patchification) and text tokens. The data mixture will be optimized using a DoReMi-inspired approach to balance modalities. The model's sole objective is next-token prediction across this unified token sequence, with no explicit alignment or contrastive loss. We will scale model size and data volume systematically to test if and how alignment emerges, evaluating on zero-shot retrieval and generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous test of the scaling hypothesis for emergent multimodal alignment, potentially enabling training on vastly larger, uncurated datasets. It could redefine data requirements for multimodal AI and offer insights into the learning dynamics of unified token spaces.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses separate encoders or aligned data, this proposal removes alignment entirely, pushing the 'single objective' paradigm from unimodal scaling (e.g., GPT) into a truly unaligned multimodal setting, a radical departure from established multimodal pretraining recipes.

proposals.title: Scaling and Data Mixtures for Zero-Shot Cross-Modal Reasoning: A Systematic Study of Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Models like SimVLM demonstrate impressive zero-shot capabilities by unifying vision and language in a single generative framework. However, the precise factors enabling their zero-shot reasoning and cross-modality transfer remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to systematically improve them. This investigation is crucial for moving beyond task-specific fine-tuning towards more general and efficient AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the zero-shot reasoning and cross-modal transfer abilities of unified generative models are primarily governed by the scale of the model and the composition of the pretraining data mixture, rather than architectural nuances alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scaling study using the SimVLM architecture, varying model size (parameters) and pretraining data mixtures. Inspired by DoReMi, we will use a proxy model to optimize the data domain weights (e.g., text, image-text pairs, synthetic data) for maximizing zero-shot performance on a held-out suite of vision-language reasoning tasks. Evaluation will rigorously benchmark zero-shot performance on diverse tasks from VQA, image captioning, and novel cross-modal inference tasks derived from datasets like NLVR2 and GQA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of how data mixture optimization and scaling laws interact to produce zero-shot capabilities in vision-language models. It will yield actionable insights for designing more data-efficient pretraining recipes and establish clearer benchmarks for evaluating emergent cross-modal reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling and data mixture are studied separately in language models, their joint, causal effect on zero-shot cross-modal transfer in unified generative architectures like SimVLM is unexplored. This moves beyond simply scaling parameters to strategically scaling data quality and diversity.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Weakly Supervised Multimodal Pretraining: A Joint Investigation of Model and Data Scaling
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for language models are well-established, but the scaling behavior of multimodal models, particularly those trained on massive, noisy, web-scale datasets, remains poorly understood. As models and datasets grow beyond current benchmarks, it is critical to predict performance limits and resource requirements to guide efficient research and deployment. This work directly addresses the core scaling themes in Yu's prior research on image pretraining and data mixture optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the scaling laws for large multimodal (image-text) transformers, when trained on weakly supervised data, will reveal a predictable but distinct power-law relationship compared to pure language models, with data quality and mixture playing a more critical role at extreme scales.
proposals.method: We will extend the DoReMi framework to optimize data mixtures for a joint image-text next-token/pixel prediction objective, scaling a decoder-only transformer (e.g., a PaLM-style architecture adapted for images) from 10B to ~500B parameters. Training will use curated but noisy web-scale datasets (e.g., LAION-5B subsets, Common Crawl) at scales up to 50 trillion tokens and 100 billion images. We will systematically vary model size, dataset size, and mixture weights to fit separate scaling laws for vision and language downstream tasks, analyzing where performance plateaus.
proposals.contribution: This project will produce the first joint scaling laws for weakly supervised multimodal pretraining, providing a predictive framework for future model development. It will also deliver a state-of-the-art, publicly documented model checkpoint and a refined, optimized data mixture recipe for large-scale multimodal learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws exist for language and some vision tasks, the joint optimization of model and data scaling for a next-pixel/token prediction objective on heterogeneous, web-scale data is novel. This integrates Yu's expertise in data mixture optimization (DoReMi) and generative image pretraining scaling into a unified, extreme-scale investigation.

proposals.title: DoReMi for Vision-Language: Optimizing Prefix-LM Training Efficiency via Dynamic Data Mixture Policies
proposals.motivation: Vision-Language Pretraining (VLP) using prefix language modeling (e.g., BLIP-2, Flamingo) is computationally intensive, scaling model and data size for improved performance. However, pre-2026 work shows that scaling data indiscriminately is suboptimal; the data mixture itself is a critical efficiency lever, as demonstrated for pure language models by DoReMi. The efficiency of the prefix-LM objective, which must process both visual tokens and a language prefix, remains underexplored from a data-centric perspective.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically optimizing the mixture of image-text data domains during prefix-LM training, using a domain-reweighting policy inspired by DoReMi, will significantly improve training efficiency and final model performance compared to static, heuristically defined mixtures.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework to the multimodal setting. A small reference model will be trained on a baseline heuristic data mixture. A larger proxy model will then train with a domain-weighted loss, where a trainable policy network adjusts weights for domains (e.g., web captions, OCR-rich images, detailed diagrams) to minimize the loss on a held-out validation set. This policy will be updated periodically, creating a dynamic curriculum. We will implement this within a standard prefix-LM architecture (e.g., a frozen visual encoder, a Q-Former, and a language model) and evaluate on downstream vision-language tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of data mixture optimization for prefix-LM VLP, delivering a concrete method to improve training efficiency. It will yield insights into which visual-linguistic domains are most valuable at different training stages and establish a data-centric scaling law for multimodal models, complementing existing work on architectural and objective scaling.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi is established for language, its application to the structured, two-stream prefix-LM objective in VLP is novel. The core challenge is defining meaningful 'domains' for multimodal data and effectively balancing the learning signals from heterogeneous visual and textual prefixes, moving beyond scaling model size alone.

proposals.title: Scaling Vision-Language Prefix LMs with Sparse Mixture-of-Experts: An Efficiency and Performance Study
proposals.motivation: Scaling dense models for Vision-Language Pretraining (VLP) is increasingly costly, with compute and memory demands growing quadratically with parameter count. Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models, as demonstrated in pure language tasks (e.g., GLaM, Switch Transformers), offer a path to scale parameters efficiently while maintaining manageable training and inference FLOPs. However, their application to prefix (encoder-decoder) VLP architectures, which must jointly process and align image and text modalities, remains underexplored and presents unique challenges for expert routing and task performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sparse MoE architecture, applied to the feed-forward layers of a prefix VLP model, can achieve superior scaling efficiency—reaching higher parameter counts and better performance on diverse vision-language tasks—compared to a dense model of equivalent training FLOPs, without sacrificing the model's few-shot and zero-shot generalization capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based prefix LM (e.g., a ViT-G/FLAN-T5-style encoder-decoder) by replacing dense feed-forward networks (FFNs) with sparse MoE layers, using a top-k gating mechanism. We will implement and compare different routing strategies, including modality-agnostic and modality-aware experts, to handle the heterogeneous input. The model will be pretrained on a large-scale image-text corpus (e.g., a filtered LAION/COYO mix) using a contrastive and generative prefix LM loss. We will rigorously evaluate scaling laws, comparing MoE variants against dense baselines on tasks like VQA, image captioning, and few-shot transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of MoE scaling laws for prefix VLP models, quantifying the trade-offs between parameter count, compute, and multimodal task performance. It will deliver a set of best practices for routing and training MoE-VLP models and release a high-performing, efficiently scalable model checkpoint, advancing the frontier of cost-effective multimodal AI.
proposals.novelty: While MoE is established in NLP, its application to the specific constraints of prefix VLP architectures—where experts must route across fused image-text representations—is novel. This work uniquely investigates whether the data mixture optimization principles from DoReMi can be extended to multimodal expert routing for improved task performance.

proposals.title: Scaling Autoregressive Image Generation via Task-Inverted Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current text-to-image models, such as diffusion models, rely on specialized architectures and training objectives distinct from large language models (LLMs). This divergence limits the direct application of powerful scaling laws and data-mixture optimization techniques proven in language modeling to image generation. A unified, autoregressive framework capable of both language and high-fidelity image generation would enable the transfer of scaling insights and efficiency gains from the language domain.
proposals.hypothesis: A decoder-only transformer, pretrained on a large-scale, interleaved corpus of text and images with a next-token (pixel) prediction objective, can be effectively adapted for high-quality text-conditioned image generation by inverting the prefix-task relationship used during language modeling, treating the text as a fixed prefix for image token generation.
proposals.method: We will scale a decoder-only transformer architecture, similar to GPT-4 or PaLM, trained autoregressively on a tokenized mixture of web text and images (using VQ-VAE or a patch-based tokenizer). Building on the DoReMi method, we will optimize the data mixture for balanced text and image modeling performance. For the inverse task, we will fine-tune the model using a modified causal attention mask that allows full attention to the textual prefix while autoregressively generating the subsequent image tokens. We will rigorously evaluate scaling trends in model size and data mixture against benchmarks like MS-COCO for fidelity and text alignment.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate the feasibility of a unified, scaling-optimized autoregressive model for both language and image generation. It will provide empirical scaling laws for this hybrid modality and establish a methodology for transferring data optimization techniques (DoReMi) from language to vision. The resulting model serves as a strong baseline for studying fundamental multi-modal scaling.
proposals.novelty: While autoregressive image generation exists, the systematic inversion of the text-prefix task within a single, scaling-optimized transformer trained on an optimized mixture of text and images is unexplored. This approach directly challenges the pre-2026 assumption that high-quality text-to-image synthesis requires specialized, non-autoregressive architectures.

proposals.title: Scaling SimVLM to Ultra-Long Sequences: Training and Evaluation for Dense Narrative Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are primarily trained and evaluated on short image-text pairs, limiting their ability to understand long-form, multi-image narratives or dense documents. As real-world applications like medical report generation, scientific paper analysis, and long-form video description demand coherent reasoning over extended multimodal sequences, scaling sequence length is a critical frontier. This work directly addresses the scaling laws for sequence length in VLMs, an underexplored axis compared to model size and dataset scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by scaling the effective sequence length during pretraining, the SimVLM architecture can develop significantly improved capabilities for dense captioning and long document understanding, and that a data mixture optimization strategy akin to DoReMi can efficiently identify the most valuable long-sequence training data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SimVLM architecture, a simple and scalable encoder-decoder model, to handle ultra-long sequences via efficient attention mechanisms (e.g., Perceiver-style cross-attention or sparse transformers). We will construct a large-scale dataset of long image-text sequences, such as interleaved comic strips, instructional videos as frame sequences, and scanned document pages. Inspired by DoReMi, we will use a small proxy model to optimize the mixture weights of different long-sequence data sources to maximize training efficiency and downstream task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of sequence-length scaling for VLMs, establishing new benchmarks for long-form multimodal understanding. It will deliver a publicly available model and dataset for long-sequence VLM tasks, advancing capabilities in areas like visual storytelling and complex document QA. The integration of data mixture optimization will also offer insights into the most valuable data distributions for training long-context models.
proposals.novelty: While scaling model and dataset size is well-studied, targeted scaling of sequence length for VLMs is novel. Combining long-sequence SimVLM training with data mixture optimization, a technique proven for language models, represents a non-obvious and efficient approach to this challenging problem.

proposals.title: Beyond the Few-Shot Mirage: A Systematic Audit of Model Capabilities Under Distribution and Task Perturbation
proposals.motivation: The field heavily relies on zero- and few-shot evaluation on static benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-bench) to measure and compare model capabilities, especially for large language models. This practice assumes these evaluations are robust proxies for general task performance and robustness. However, pre-2026 work, including findings that 'Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet,' suggests a potential disconnect between few-shot performance and reliable, generalizable capability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that zero/few-shot performance on standard benchmarks provides a misleadingly optimistic and incomplete picture of model capabilities, failing to predict performance under realistic distribution shifts, novel task formulations, or when models are required to execute multi-step reasoning without in-context scaffolding.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled audit across models of varying scales (inspired by scaling themes). For a fixed set of core capabilities (e.g., reasoning, knowledge retrieval), we will create evaluation suites with systematic perturbations: (1) distribution shifts in input style and domain, (2) deconstruction of tasks into subtasks not seen in-context, and (3) 'antagonistic' few-shot prompts designed to mislead. We will measure the performance gap between standard few-shot and these perturbed settings, analyzing the correlation with model size, architecture, and training data mixture (leveraging DoReMi-inspired analysis).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for auditing the robustness of few-shot evaluation, quantifying its limitations. It will challenge the community's over-reliance on these metrics for model comparison and scaling laws, pushing for more robust evaluation protocols that better reflect real-world deployment challenges.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes specific failures, our approach systematically generates and measures the *gap* between curated few-shot performance and performance under controlled perturbations, directly testing the sufficiency of the dominant evaluation paradigm. It connects scaling trends to evaluation robustness, a link not deeply explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Sparse Mixtures of Experts for Efficient Scaling in Multimodal Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Large multimodal models (LMMs) combining vision and language are scaling rapidly, but their dense architectures lead to prohibitive computational costs for both training and inference. The mixture-of-experts (MoE) paradigm has proven highly effective for scaling language models efficiently, yet its application to multimodal learning remains largely unexplored. This work addresses the critical need for scalable and efficient architectures for next-generation vision-language tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sparsely activated MoE architecture, strategically applied to the multimodal fusion components of a model, can achieve superior performance and scaling efficiency compared to dense counterparts of equivalent parameter count, without a proportional increase in computational cost.
proposals.method: We will design and train a large-scale vision-language model based on a transformer architecture. The core innovation is integrating MoE layers into the multimodal fusion transformer that processes concatenated image and text embeddings. We will explore expert specialization strategies (e.g., modality-specific, concept-specific) and routing algorithms (e.g., a top-k gating network) to activate only a subset of experts per token. Training will leverage large-scale datasets like LAION and follow a two-stage recipe: initial contrastive pretraining of encoders, followed by MoE-enhanced multimodal generative pretraining, evaluating on benchmarks like VQAv2 and OK-VQA.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first comprehensive analysis of MoE scaling laws for multimodal models, providing a blueprint for building more capable and efficient LMMs. It will also release a novel model architecture and training recipe, advancing the field beyond simply scaling dense models.
proposals.novelty: While MoE is established in unimodal NLP, its principled extension to the heterogeneous, cross-modal interactions central to vision-language understanding is novel. This moves beyond simply placing MoE in the vision encoder or language decoder to actively sparsifying the multimodal fusion process itself.

proposals.title: Scaling Down to Speed Up: A Data-Centric Optimization of Non-Recurrent Architectures for Latency-Critical Inference
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models in real-time applications like translation, voice assistants, and interactive agents is bottlenecked by inference latency. While non-recurrent architectures (e.g., Transformers) have superior accuracy, their autoregressive decoding remains computationally heavy. Pre-2026 scaling efforts have focused on training larger models, but there is a critical need for post-training optimization that preserves accuracy while drastically reducing per-token latency for deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synergistic optimization of a non-recurrent model's architecture and its inference-time data distribution—informed by training data mixture principles—can achieve significantly lower latency without sacrificing task performance, moving beyond isolated techniques like quantization or pruning.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DoReMi framework to identify and synthesize a 'latency-critical' data mixture that emphasizes examples where the model currently exhibits high uncertainty or slow decoding. Using this curated dataset, we will apply a structured, progressive pruning and distillation regimen targeting the attention mechanism and feed-forward layers of a pre-trained Transformer. The optimization will be guided by a novel latency-aware loss function that directly penalizes per-token generation time during fine-tuning, creating a feedback loop between data, architecture, and measured inference speed.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a methodology for co-optimizing data and architecture for fast inference, a set of benchmark results on latency-critical tasks, and an open-source, optimized model checkpoint. It will provide a principled, data-driven path to efficient deployment, complementing the field's focus on scaling up training.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating data mixture optimization (DoReMi) directly into the inference-speed optimization loop, rather than treating architecture compression and data selection as separate problems. This creates a targeted, performance-preserving method distinct from generic model distillation or hardware-aware compilation.

proposals.title: NAS-QA: Neural Architecture Search for Hybrid Convolution-Attention Blocks in Question Answering
proposals.motivation: Transformer-based models dominate QA but are computationally expensive for long-context tasks, while pure convolutional models lack the global reasoning needed for complex QA. The optimal hybrid architecture balancing local feature extraction (convolution) and global dependency modeling (attention) for QA remains an open, manually tuned problem. Automating this search is critical for building efficient, high-performance QA systems, aligning with the scaling and performance themes in pre-2026 research.
proposals.hypothesis: A differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) framework can discover novel, task-optimal hybrid blocks of convolution and attention operations that outperform manually designed baselines (e.g., pure transformers or simple hybrids) on extractive and multi-choice QA tasks, especially in few-shot and compute-limited settings.
proposals.method: We will design a fine-grained, cell-based search space where each block is a directed acyclic graph of candidate operations (e.g., multi-head self-attention, depthwise convolution, feed-forward layers). We will employ a one-shot, differentiable NAS approach (e.g., DARTS) to efficiently search over this space on a proxy QA dataset (e.g., SQuAD). The discovered architecture will then be scaled up and trained from scratch on larger QA benchmarks (e.g., Natural Questions, RACE), with analysis guided by data mixture optimization principles from DoReMi to ensure robust training.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, NAS-derived architecture template for QA that provides a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off than standard transformers. It will also provide insights into the fundamental inductive biases required for language understanding tasks, moving beyond scaling model size alone. The resulting methodology will offer a blueprint for automating architecture design for other language performance tasks.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been applied to vision and simple NLP architectures, a targeted search for optimal convolution-attention hybrids specifically for the structured reasoning demands of QA is unexplored. This moves beyond scaling existing architectures or optimizing data mixtures (DoReMi) to co-optimizing the fundamental computational primitives of the model itself for a task family.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Synthetic Data: Can Self-Training on Trillions of Machine-Generated Tokens Break the Data Bottleneck?
proposals.motivation: The scaling of large language models is fundamentally constrained by the quantity and quality of available human-generated text data. As models grow larger, they risk under-training due to this data bottleneck, limiting performance gains. Synthetic data generation offers a potential solution, but its scaling properties, quality dynamics, and ultimate effectiveness for pre-training remain poorly understood and are a critical open question in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-training loop, where a large language model generates massive, diverse synthetic data which is then used for further pre-training, can lead to significant performance improvements beyond scaling on human data alone, provided the data mixture is carefully optimized to maintain quality and diversity.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework to optimize the mixture of synthetic data domains generated by a large, frozen base model (e.g., a 70B parameter LLM). This generator will produce orders of magnitude more tokens than the original human dataset. A smaller proxy model will be trained on these synthetic mixtures, with its performance on a held-out validation set guiding domain weight updates via distributionally robust optimization. The final optimized synthetic data mixture will then be used to pre-train a large target model from scratch, and its performance will be compared against a baseline model trained only on the original human data at equivalent compute budgets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous empirical study of scaling laws for synthetic pre-training data, quantifying the performance returns from massive self-generated corpora. It will yield practical algorithms for synthetic data mixture optimization and establish whether synthetic data can truly circumvent the human data bottleneck for next-generation model scaling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses synthetic data for fine-tuning or distillation, we propose its use for large-scale *pre-training*, directly addressing the core data scarcity problem. The key novelty is combining the scale of synthetic data generation with the data mixture optimization principles of DoReMi to actively manage quality degradation, a non-obvious extension beyond curating static human datasets.

proposals.title: Scaling Multi-Task QA: A Data Mixture Optimization and Scaling Law Analysis for Unified Question Answering
proposals.motivation: The landscape of question answering (QA) is fragmented into numerous specialized benchmarks (e.g., SQuAD, HotpotQA, TriviaQA), each probing different skills. Training separate models for each is inefficient and fails to leverage potential synergies. Joint training on all available QA data presents a significant scaling challenge, requiring methods to manage data heterogeneity and optimize for efficient multi-task learning, a core theme in the researcher's prior work on language, tasks, and scaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying data mixture optimization techniques (like DoReMi) to a massive, unified QA corpus, we can train a single model that achieves superior performance and data efficiency across all constituent benchmarks compared to individually trained or naively mixed baselines, and that the scaling laws for this multi-task setting will differ from standard single-task language model pretraining.
proposals.method: We will aggregate a large-scale, diverse QA dataset from all major public benchmarks (pre-2026). We will adapt the DoReMi framework to optimize the mixture weights of these QA subsets during joint training of a T5-style or decoder-only language model. The training objective will be a unified sequence-to-sequence QA formulation. We will systematically vary model size and compute budget to derive scaling laws for multi-task QA performance, analyzing metrics like aggregate accuracy, per-dataset performance, and negative transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a state-of-the-art, general-purpose QA model and the optimized data mixture recipe for multi-task QA, 2) a novel empirical analysis of scaling laws in a large-scale, heterogeneous multi-task setting, and 3) insights into the transfer and interference dynamics between different QA tasks, informing future benchmark and model design.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is well-studied, the systematic application of data mixture optimization and scaling law analysis to the entire, massive QA benchmark ecosystem is novel. It directly tests whether scaling compute and model size, guided by optimized data proportions, can overcome the traditional challenges of negative transfer in highly heterogeneous task collections.

proposals.title: DoReMi for the Edge: Data Mixture Optimization for Extremely Small Language Models
proposals.motivation: The scaling paradigm has focused on improving performance by increasing model size, but deployment on edge devices and in cost-sensitive applications requires highly capable, tiny models (<100M parameters). Pre-2026 work like DoReMi shows that optimizing data mixtures is crucial for scaling large models, but its principles have not been systematically applied under severe compute and parameter constraints, where data quality and curriculum are likely even more critical.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying and adapting the DoReMi (Domain Reweighting with Minimax Optimization) framework to the small-model regime will yield optimal, non-intuitive data mixtures that significantly improve the performance-per-parameter of sub-100M parameter models, outperforming standard web-scale mixtures.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi algorithm, using a small reference model (e.g., 100M params) as the proxy. We will train a series of extremely small target models (e.g., 10M, 30M, 70M params) on mixtures reweighted by the proxy to minimize worst-domain loss. The training will use a fixed, small compute budget per model, simulating edge-device constraints. We will benchmark performance on diverse downstream tasks (classification, generation, reasoning) against baselines trained on uniform or heuristically filtered data.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a methodology for data-centric optimization of tiny models, providing a counterpoint to pure architectural scaling. We will release optimized data mixture recipes for common small-model architectures and demonstrate that superior data selection can compensate for an order-of-magnitude reduction in parameters, making high-performance NLP more accessible.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi optimized mixtures for scaling large models, applying its minimax optimization to find mixtures specifically for *extremely* small models is unexplored. This inverts the scaling narrative, asking how to scale *down* efficiently, which is non-trivial as the optimal data for a 10B parameter model is likely suboptimal for a 10M parameter model.

proposals.title: Learning to Sample: Dynamic Task Mixture Optimization via Online Learning Progress Estimation
proposals.motivation: Current large-scale multi-task and continual learning pipelines rely on static data mixtures, as exemplified by DoReMi, which are optimized once before training. This is suboptimal as a model's learning dynamics change throughout training; a task's utility for overall loss reduction is not constant. An online, adaptive sampling strategy could dramatically improve compute and data efficiency, a critical scaling concern for large models.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic task sampling policy, which allocates compute based on real-time estimates of per-task learning progress (expected loss reduction), will outperform static mixture strategies in terms of final multi-task performance and convergence speed across diverse benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will formulate task sampling as a multi-armed bandit problem where each arm is a task. The reward for sampling a task is defined as the reduction in its loss over a short horizon, estimated using a small held-out validation set or via an online approximation of the loss gradient's norm. We will implement and compare several bandit algorithms (e.g., Upper Confidence Bound, Thompson Sampling) to select tasks during training. The system will be integrated into a standard Transformer training loop and evaluated on established multi-task NLP benchmarks (e.g., SuperGLUE, MMLU) and continual learning sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, online alternative to static data mixture optimization, directly linking scaling efficiency to adaptive resource allocation. It will yield a general framework for dynamic curriculum learning and offer new insights into the temporal dynamics of multi-task training.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi optimizes a static mixture pre-training, our method continuously adapts the sampling distribution *during* training based on the model's instantaneous state. This moves beyond one-shot optimization to a closed-loop, model-aware control system for training data.

proposals.title: Adversarial Instruction Distillation: Growing a Minimal, High-Leverage Curriculum for Compositional Generalization
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for aligning large language models (LLMs) with user intent, but current methods rely on static, often massive, datasets (e.g., FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions). This static approach is inefficient and may fail to systematically cover the vast, compositional space of possible instructions, leading to brittle generalization as noted in recent work on LLMs' inability to self-correct. A dynamic, data-efficient method for generating a maximally effective instruction set is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial, iterative process between a generator and a critic can distill a compact, high-quality instruction set that forces the model to learn fundamental compositional skills, leading to superior generalization on unseen, complex tasks compared to models trained on static, larger datasets.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-model system: a student LLM (the generator) and a critic LLM. Starting from a small seed of instructions, the critic will propose adversarial examples—novel phrasings, task compositions, and edge cases—that expose the student's failures. Using a reward model trained on correctness and diversity, we will selectively add the most challenging instructions to the training set. This process iterates, inspired by DoReMi's data mixture optimization but applied to instruction generation, creating a dynamically growing curriculum. We will evaluate on held-out, compositionally complex benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient instruction tuning, moving beyond static datasets. It will yield a method to create compact, high-leverage instruction sets and offer empirical evidence on the relationship between adversarial curriculum design and emergent compositional generalization in LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and data curation exist, applying an iterative adversarial distillation loop specifically to grow an instruction-tuning curriculum for compositional generalization is novel. It directly challenges the prevailing 'scale-up the dataset' paradigm by focusing on dynamic, targeted data generation.

proposals.title: SkillPrimitives-IT: Scaling Instruction Following via Composition of Foundational Cognitive Primitives
proposals.motivation: Current instruction tuning relies on massive, manually curated datasets of thousands of tasks, which is costly and may not guarantee robust generalization. This approach mirrors the inefficiency of training a robot on every possible motion rather than on fundamental motor skills. A more data-efficient and theoretically grounded paradigm, inspired by skill composition in robotics and developmental learning, is needed to achieve scalable and generalizable instruction-following capabilities in language models.
proposals.hypothesis: Intensive instruction tuning on a minimal, systematically identified set of foundational cognitive skill primitives (e.g., reformulation, comparison, deduction) will enable a language model to achieve superior combinatorial generalization to novel, complex instructions, outperforming models tuned on a larger but less structured set of tasks.
proposals.method: We will first induce a candidate set of skill primitives through a combination of mechanistic analysis of model failures (inspired by 'self-correction' studies) and clustering of tasks in existing instruction datasets (e.g., FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions) by their underlying reasoning types. We will then create a high-quality, scaled dataset for each primitive using synthetic generation and curation. A base model (e.g., a pretrained T5 or LLaMA variant) will be instruction-tuned exclusively on this primitive dataset. Evaluation will involve systematic testing on held-out primitive combinations, existing instruction benchmarks, and novel user queries, with a focus on zero-shot generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient instruction tuning, potentially reducing data curation needs. It will yield a publicly available taxonomy and dataset of cognitive skill primitives and demonstrate whether mastering compositional building blocks is a more effective path to general instruction following than task diversity alone.
proposals.novelty: While data mixture optimization (DoReMi) and scaling analyses are common, applying the concept of reusable motor primitives from robotics to structure the objective of instruction tuning is a novel cross-disciplinary analogy. It shifts the focus from scaling task diversity to scaling mastery of fundamental skills.

proposals.title: The Instruction Genome: Pareto-Optimal Instruction Tuning via Task Ablation and Compute-Aware Curriculum
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for aligning large language models, but current practice uses thousands of tasks without rigorous compute efficiency analysis. Given the high cost of scaling and the findings from DoReMi on data mixture optimization, there is a clear need to determine the minimal, highest-leverage instruction set and its optimal training schedule under a fixed compute budget to maximize downstream zero-shot performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A joint optimization of task set selection (ablation) and compute-aware curriculum learning can identify a Pareto-optimal frontier between instruction diversity and compute allocation, yielding superior generalization per FLOP compared to static, large-scale instruction tuning.
proposals.method: We will first apply a gradient-based ablation method, inspired by data pruning and DoReMi's distributional robust optimization, to a massive seed set (e.g., Flan, Super-NaturalInstructions) to extract a minimal 'core' task set maximizing latent skill coverage. Second, under a strict parameter update budget, we will implement a dynamic curriculum scheduler that allocates more compute (steps, batch size) to the core set early, then fine-tunes allocation across remaining tasks based on per-task learning progress metrics, creating a compute-aware training trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for compute-optimal instruction tuning, delivering a method to discover minimal high-impact task sets and their optimal training schedules. It will yield concrete Pareto curves to guide efficient model alignment and potentially a released 'instruction genome' for popular model families.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales data mixtures or uses static curricula, the joint optimization of task ablation and a compute-aware curriculum for instruction tuning under a hard budget is novel, directly linking task selection efficiency to dynamic training resource allocation.

proposals.title: Mode-Switching for Robust Reasoning: A Differential Training Paradigm to Mitigate the Over-Explanation Penalty in Chain-of-Thought Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) fine-tuning has become a standard method for enhancing LLM reasoning, but its impact on tasks requiring direct, concise answers is underexplored. Pre-2026 work, including Wei et al.'s DoReMi, shows data mixture optimization is critical for scaling, yet no work systematically examines the potential negative transfer from exclusive CoT training. This research addresses a key gap in understanding task-specific performance trade-offs in instruction tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: Exclusive fine-tuning on detailed CoT data creates an 'over-explanation penalty,' degrading performance on direct-answer tasks, but a differentially trained model that learns to switch between reasoning and direct modes based on contextual cues can eliminate this penalty.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled dataset with parallel examples: each question paired with both a CoT solution and a direct answer, tagged with explicit mode tokens (e.g., [REASON] and [DIRECT]). We will fine-tune a base language model (e.g., a scaled-down version of models studied in Wei's prior scaling work) on three mixtures: CoT-only, Direct-only, and the mixed differential dataset. Performance will be evaluated on held-out tasks from benchmarks like GSM8K (for reasoning) and TriviaQA (for direct recall), using both standard and mode-specific prompts to test switching capability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence of a CoT-induced performance penalty on direct tasks and demonstrate a practical training solution. It will contribute a novel framework for data mixture design that optimizes for multi-format task performance, advancing beyond simple scaling or uniform data weighting strategies.
proposals.novelty: The analogy to motor learning 'choking' and the explicit test of CoT's negative effects are novel. While data mixture optimization exists (DoReMi), applying it to control *reasoning style* as a switchable mode based on prompt context is a new direction for efficient model specialization.

proposals.title: Task-Conditioned Reasoning Formats: Optimizing Minimal Instruction-Tuning Curricula for Accuracy and Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for aligning LLMs, but creating effective datasets is resource-intensive. Prior work on data mixture optimization (e.g., DoReMi) and scaling has focused on pretraining or large-scale fine-tuning, leaving a gap in understanding how to construct minimal, high-impact instruction sets. The uncritical application of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to all tasks may introduce inefficiencies or harm performance on tasks requiring direct, concise answers, a problem highlighted by findings that LLMs struggle with self-correction.
proposals.hypothesis: For a minimal set of core tasks, generalization is maximized by jointly optimizing for task selection and the assignment of task-specific reasoning formats (CoT or direct), and that strategically withholding CoT for inherently concise tasks prevents performance degradation within a compact curriculum.
proposals.method: We will first establish a taxonomy of tasks (e.g., arithmetic, fact retrieval, logical inference) and characterize their inherent reasoning format preference. Using a base model (e.g., a scaled-down variant of models studied in 'Rethinking generative image pretraining'), we will apply a data-efficient optimization loop inspired by DoReMi's distribution matching. This loop will jointly learn weights for both task selection and format assignment, minimizing a loss that contrasts performance on a broad validation set. Experiments will measure interference and efficiency across curricula of varying sizes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for building minimal, high-performance instruction-tuning datasets, reducing data and compute costs. It will yield concrete guidelines on when and where to apply CoT reasoning, advancing beyond one-size-fits-all prompting strategies. The resulting 'task-conditioned' curriculum methodology will be a step toward more efficient model specialization.
proposals.novelty: While data mixture optimization and CoT are well-studied in isolation, their joint optimization for minimal instruction sets is unexplored. The key insight is that the reasoning format is a critical, tunable dimension of the data curriculum itself, not just an inference-time technique.

proposals.title: Format Transfer as a Diagnostic for Procedural vs. Declarative Learning in Instruction-Tuned LLMs
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is a dominant paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, yet its underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. It is unclear whether improvements stem from acquiring new declarative knowledge about tasks or from learning a generalized procedural skill for following instructions. Disentangling these is crucial for efficient scaling and targeted model improvement, as it informs whether to invest in more data, better formats, or novel tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: The primary benefit of instruction tuning is the acquisition of a generalized, format-agnostic procedure for interpreting and executing instructions, rather than the accumulation of specific, declarative knowledge for solving the tasks presented during tuning.
proposals.method: We will instruction-tune a base model (e.g., a LLaMA or T5 variant) on a diverse corpus of tasks (e.g., from FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions) using a single, consistent instructional format (Format A). We will then evaluate on a controlled test suite comprising: (1) the same tasks presented in a novel, unseen format (Format B), and (2) entirely novel tasks not seen during tuning. A strong dissociation—significant performance gains on (1) but not on (2)—would support the procedural learning hypothesis. We will analyze scaling effects by repeating the experiment across model sizes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence distinguishing procedural from declarative learning in instruction tuning, offering a new diagnostic framework. It will yield actionable insights for data mixture optimization (as in DoReMi) and challenge assumptions about knowledge transfer in scaling, potentially explaining why models can fail to self-correct reasoning despite instruction tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies instruction tuning's overall effectiveness, our 'format transfer' experiment, inspired by cognitive psychology, is a novel operationalization for isolating learning mechanisms. This moves beyond correlational performance analysis to a causal, disentangled evaluation of what models actually learn.

proposals.title: Instruction Data Causal Attribution: A Controlled Generation Framework for Isolating the Impact of Data Attributes on Model Capabilities
proposals.motivation: Current instruction-tuning practices rely on vague heuristics of 'data quality,' leading to inefficient scaling and unpredictable model behaviors. As shown in works like DoReMi, data mixture optimization is crucial, but we lack a principled understanding of which specific attributes in instruction data causally influence which final model capabilities. This research aims to replace intuition with evidence, providing actionable guidelines for data curation to achieve targeted performance improvements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that orthogonal, measurable attributes of instruction data (e.g., syntactic complexity, reasoning depth, factual density) have distinct, quantifiable causal effects on specific downstream capabilities (e.g., multi-step reasoning, factual accuracy, instruction following), and that these effects can be isolated through controlled dataset generation and causal mediation analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework to generate synthetic instruction-tuning datasets using a large language model (e.g., GPT-4) where key attributes are systematically manipulated via controlled prompting and filtering. We will train a series of medium-scale language models (e.g., 1-3B parameters, Pythia-style) on these controlled datasets. Their performance will be evaluated on held-out benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BBH, instruction-following tasks) and analyzed using causal mediation techniques to attribute performance variance to specific data attributes, controlling for scaling effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal map linking specific, measurable instruction data attributes to emergent model capabilities. It will deliver a practical framework for diagnosing capability gaps and generating targeted data for remediation, moving beyond trial-and-error scaling. The resulting insights will inform more efficient data curation and mixture strategies for instruction tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work scales data mixtures (DoReMi) or critiques model self-correction, this proposal uniquely focuses on *decomposing* data quality into orthogonal attributes and establishing *causal*, not just correlational, links to capabilities through controlled synthetic data generation, a pre-2026 underexplored approach.

proposals.title: Instruction Data Quality Assessment via Targeted Forgetting and Persistence Metrics
proposals.motivation: Scaling instruction-tuned models relies on large, often noisy datasets, but the quality of individual instructions is poorly understood. Current methods for data selection, like DoReMi, optimize mixtures but lack a direct, post-hoc metric for the intrinsic value of a specific instruction. A principled method to identify a minimal, high-quality instruction set is needed to improve data efficiency and model performance.
proposals.hypothesis: The persistence of a learned behavior after targeted un-tuning on its specific training data is a robust, operational metric for the quality and impact of that instruction data, enabling the identification of a core, high-quality subset.
proposals.method: We will first train a base language model (e.g., a T5 or GPT-2 variant) on a large, mixed-quality instruction dataset. We will then apply gradient-based targeted forgetting algorithms, inspired by un-learning techniques for model editing, to selectively 'un-tune' the model on candidate instruction subsets. By measuring the degradation in performance on held-out tasks after each targeted forgetting operation, we will compute a 'persistence score' for each data subset. This score will guide an iterative pruning algorithm to construct a minimal, high-quality instruction set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, model-intrinsic metric for instruction data quality, moving beyond heuristics. It will yield a methodology for distilling large instruction datasets into high-impact cores and contribute to understanding the differential memorization and generalization of instructions in language models.
proposals.novelty: It repurposes model forgetting, typically seen as a bug or privacy tool, as an active, closed-loop probe for data curation. This inverts the standard scaling paradigm by using removal, not addition, to assess data value.

proposals.title: DoReMi for Instruction Tuning: Optimizing Data Mixtures to Prevent Catastrophic Forgetting in Domain Specialists
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for adapting large language models to specialized domains, but it often induces catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities and safety guardrails. This creates a trade-off between domain expertise and model robustness, limiting the deployment of reliable specialist assistants. Pre-2026, methods like continual learning and replay buffers have shown limited success at scale, and data mixture optimization has not been systematically applied to this instruction-tuning problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamically optimizing the mixture of domain-specific and general-purpose instruction data during training—using a performance-based weighting scheme—can minimize catastrophic forgetting without compromising domain performance. This will yield a model that is both a domain expert and a robust, safe assistant.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi (Domain Reweighting with Minimax Optimization) framework to instruction tuning. A small proxy model will iteratively adjust the mixture weights between a target domain dataset (e.g., medical or legal instructions) and a sustained stream of general tasks and safety benchmarks (e.g., FLAN collections, Anthropic's HH-RLHF). The optimization objective will minimize the worst-case loss across all task groups. Training will be performed on a large, instruction-tuned base model (e.g., a T5 or LLaMA variant), with evaluation guided by a suite monitoring domain accuracy, general task performance, and safety benchmark scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, data-centric methodology for creating robust domain-specialist models. It will yield empirical results on the data mixtures required to balance specialization and generalization, contributing a new benchmark for evaluating catastrophic forgetting in instruction-tuned models. The resulting models and mixture schedules will be released as a public resource.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi was applied to pretraining, its application to the instruction-tuning phase for catastrophic forgetting mitigation is novel. The approach moves beyond static replay or regularization by using dynamic, optimization-driven data scheduling specifically for the multi-domain instruction setting.

proposals.title: MoRA: Mixture of Robust Adapters for Compositional Multi-Task Instruction Tuning
proposals.motivation: Scaling instruction tuning to thousands of diverse tasks is hindered by catastrophic interference, where learning new tasks degrades performance on old ones. Existing Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA use a single set of adapted parameters, which becomes a bottleneck for multi-task scaling and compositional generalization. This work addresses the core challenge of efficiently and robustly composing skills within a frozen large language model.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, sparse mixture of task-specialized PEFT modules, selected per instruction by a learned router and regularized for orthogonality, will mitigate interference and enable robust compositional generalization better than monolithic PEFT approaches.
proposals.method: We propose a Mixture of Robust Adapters (MoRA) framework. Each expert is a low-rank LoRA-style module. A lightweight router network, conditioned on the instruction embedding, computes a sparse gating function to activate 1-2 experts per forward pass. To enforce diversity and stability, we apply spectral regularization on the experts' parameter matrices to encourage orthogonality between their subspaces. Training employs a strategic data rehearsal schedule, interleaving batches from new and previously learned task clusters.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a scalable PEFT architecture for massive multi-task instruction tuning that maintains performance across tasks. It will yield new insights into mitigating interference via subspace regularization in mixture-of-experts systems. The resulting model will demonstrate improved zero-shot generalization to novel compositional instructions.
proposals.novelty: This is the first integration of a dynamic, sparse mixture-of-experts architecture specifically for PEFT, moving beyond a single adapter. The novel use of spectral regularization to decorrelate expert subspaces directly targets the interference problem in a parameter-efficient context.

proposals.title: Scaling Instruction-Tuned Specialization: A Systematic Study of Parameter-Efficient Adaptation Across Tasks and Model Sizes
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is critical for aligning large language models (LLMs) with diverse human tasks, but full fine-tuning is prohibitively expensive and leads to catastrophic forgetting. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA and adapters offer a promising alternative, but their effectiveness and scaling properties when applied to large-scale, multi-task instruction datasets remain underexplored. This research addresses the core challenge of efficiently scaling task adaptation without sacrificing performance or model stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that instruction tuning via PEFT methods can achieve performance parity with full fine-tuning on a broad suite of tasks while enabling more efficient multi-task specialization and better preservation of base model capabilities. Furthermore, we posit that the optimal PEFT configuration (e.g., rank, placement) will scale predictably with model size and task diversity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study using the T5 and LLaMA model families, scaling from hundreds of millions to tens of billions of parameters. We will instruction-tune these models on mixtures like Flan and Super-NaturalInstructions, comparing full fine-tuning against LoRA and adapter-based PEFT variants. We will systematically vary PEFT hyperparameters (e.g., LoRA rank, adapter bottleneck size) and architectural placement. Evaluation will measure performance on held-out instruction-following tasks, zero-shot generalization to new task formats, and the retention of base model knowledge and reasoning abilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven framework for selecting and configuring PEFT methods for instruction tuning, directly informing more sustainable and scalable LLM deployment. It will yield concrete scaling laws for PEFT-augmented instruction tuning, contributing to the understanding of how efficiency, model size, and task diversity interact.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT and instruction tuning are established independently, a systematic investigation of their combination—especially one that analyzes scaling laws and multi-task interference—is novel. This moves beyond demonstrating feasibility to providing prescriptive engineering and scientific insights for efficient large-scale adaptation.

proposals.title: Counterfactual Modality Balancing: An Adversarial Framework for Robust Multimodal Instruction Tuning
proposals.motivation: Large multimodal models (LMMs) for instruction following often exhibit 'modality collapse,' where they rely primarily on the language prompt and ignore visual or audio inputs, undermining their grounding in the real world. This bias is exacerbated by standard instruction-tuning datasets, which can often be solved via linguistic cues alone. Addressing this is critical for deploying reliable LMMs in complex, grounded tasks where all modalities are essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly generating and training on adversarial counterfactual examples—where a model's over-reliance on one modality leads to failure—will enforce balanced cross-modal understanding and yield a more robust multimodal instruction-following model.
proposals.method: We will extend a base LMM (e.g., LLaVA or Flamingo) with a two-stage framework. First, we generate adversarial counterfactuals by applying gradient-based attacks (e.g., projected gradient descent) to minimally perturb or ablate one input modality (e.g., blurring key image regions) to create examples solvable only with full multimodal context. Second, we introduce a Modality Disparity Loss that penalizes differences in output distributions between original and counterfactual examples, combined with standard instruction-tuning loss. Training will use a mixture of standard data (e.g., LLaVA-Instruct) and our generated adversarial set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel training framework to diagnose and mitigate modality bias in LMMs, 2) a new benchmark of adversarial counterfactuals for evaluating multimodal reliance, and 3) a more robust model that demonstrates improved performance on tasks requiring genuine cross-modal integration.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial training and data mixture optimization (DoReMi) exist, applying adversarial attacks to generate modality-specific counterfactuals for balancing multimodal understanding is a novel direction. This moves beyond simple data scaling to actively shape the model's internal reasoning pathways across modalities.

proposals.title: MSAP: Multi-Sensory Alignment Protocols for Coherent Cross-Modal Instruction Tuning
proposals.motivation: Current multimodal instruction-tuned models often exhibit modality-specific brittleness and fail to transfer semantic understanding coherently across vision, audio, and text. This limits their utility for real-world tasks requiring unified reasoning from diverse sensory inputs. Drawing inspiration from sensory substitution in cognitive neuroscience, we posit that explicit alignment protocols during instruction tuning can address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that instruction tuning a multimodal model on a dataset where each semantic task is explicitly presented with aligned inputs from multiple modalities will force the model to build a unified, task-oriented representation, thereby significantly improving cross-modal generalization and response coherence compared to standard interleaved training.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale instruction dataset where each instruction (e.g., 'describe the scene', 'infer the next action') is paired with semantically aligned triplets: an image, a descriptive audio clip, and a text caption. We will fine-tune a large-scale vision-language-audio model (e.g., a Flamingo or CM3-like architecture extended with a Perceiver-based audio encoder) on this dataset using a contrastive alignment loss alongside the standard instruction-following loss. This loss will explicitly minimize the distance between the model's internal representations for the different modalities of the same task instance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel methodology (MSAP) for structured cross-modal instruction tuning, 2) a publicly released dataset of multi-sensory instruction triplets, and 3) empirical evidence that explicit sensory alignment during tuning improves cross-modal task performance and coherence, moving beyond simple modality mixing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work mixes multimodal data, our protocol is novel in its structured, task-centric alignment of exact semantic content across three modalities during instruction tuning, directly inspired by sensory substitution principles to build a unified representation space.

proposals.title: Modality-Aware Chain-of-Thought: Learning When to Reason in Multimodal Instruction Tuning
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data has become standard for improving reasoning in language models. However, in multimodal models (e.g., vision-language, audio-language), tasks like visual recognition or audio captioning often require direct, perception-based answers, where verbose reasoning can introduce errors or inefficiencies. This creates a critical tension in data curation and model training, as indiscriminate use of CoT may degrade performance on tasks where the primary signal is non-textual.
proposals.hypothesis: Conditionally applying CoT data during multimodal instruction tuning, based on the task's required reasoning depth and dominant input modality, will yield models that outperform those trained on uniform CoT strategies, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs across diverse benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will construct a modality-annotated instruction dataset by classifying tasks from sources like VQA-v2, AudioCaps, and ALFRED as requiring 'direct perception' or 'multi-step reasoning.' We will fine-tune a pre-trained multimodal model (e.g., Flamingo or a vision-audio-language variant) using a DoReMi-inspired data mixing optimizer. This optimizer will learn a per-task-group weighting for CoT versus standard instruction data. The model's architecture will be augmented with a lightweight 'reasoning router' module that conditions the generation style on input modality and task embedding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled framework for data mixture optimization in multimodal instruction tuning, moving beyond one-size-fits-all CoT. It will deliver a new benchmark suite for evaluating reasoning-perception trade-offs and a set of models that demonstrate superior task-specific performance by dynamically modulating their reasoning process.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi optimizes data mixtures for language models, and CoT is well-studied in unimodal settings, applying modality-aware conditional data mixing to control reasoning verbosity in multimodal systems is a novel direction. It directly addresses the conflict between perceptual grounding and abstract reasoning inherent in combined vision/audio-language tasks.

proposals.title: Scaling Instruction Tuning for Multimodal Models: Data Mixture Optimization and Cross-Modal Task Generalization
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning has proven transformative for large language models (LLMs), enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks. However, its application to multimodal models (e.g., vision-language, audio-language) remains underexplored and faces unique challenges, such as aligning cross-modal representations and managing heterogeneous data. A systematic investigation into scaling instruction tuning for multimodal models is crucial for developing more capable and controllable AI assistants that can process and reason about the world beyond text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying data mixture optimization techniques, analogous to DoReMi for language, to a diverse corpus of multimodal instruction-following data will significantly improve the zero-shot performance and cross-modal task generalization of large vision-language models, outperforming standard instruction tuning with heuristically balanced data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework to optimize the mixture weights of different multimodal instruction-tuning datasets (e.g., image-text tasks, visual reasoning, audio captioning). Using a pre-trained large vision-language model (e.g., a Flamingo or BLIP-2 variant) as a proxy, we will train a small reference model with a uniform data mixture and use its per-domain losses to reweight the mixture for training a larger target model. The core architecture will be a decoder-only, modality-agnostic transformer trained with a next-token prediction objective, integrating visual/audio features via cross-attention. We will rigorously evaluate scaling laws with respect to model size and data mixture diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) the first principled method for data mixture optimization in multimodal instruction tuning, 2) a scaling analysis of instruction-tuned multimodal models, revealing the interplay between model size, data diversity, and performance on held-out cross-modal tasks, and 3) a new state-of-the-art model and a curated benchmark for evaluating multimodal instruction following and self-correction abilities.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi optimizes data mixtures for unimodal language pretraining, applying this paradigm to the heterogeneous, instruction-following phase of multimodal model training is novel. Furthermore, we explicitly test whether optimized instruction tuning can mitigate the 'self-correction' failures observed in LLMs when applied to multimodal reasoning chains.

proposals.title: DoReMi for Instructions: Scaling High-Fidelity Synthetic Data with a Learned Semantic Critic
proposals.motivation: Scaling instruction-tuned models requires massive, high-quality datasets, but manual curation is costly and limits diversity. Prior data mixture optimization work like DoReMi shows the importance of data quality for scaling, while recent findings indicate that naive synthetic data generation can poison training with logical inconsistencies and factual errors, undermining model performance and robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a semantic fidelity critic, trained to evaluate logical coherence and task alignment, can guide a data augmentation pipeline to generate synthetic instruction data that maintains high instructional integrity, thereby improving the efficiency and robustness of instruction tuning compared to scaling with unfiltered synthetic data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pipeline. First, we will apply a suite of augmentation operators (e.g., counterfactual rewriting via T5, constraint variation, style transfer) to a seed corpus of high-quality instructions (e.g., Flan collections) to generate candidate pairs. Second, we will train a separate critic model, based on a pretrained encoder like DeBERTa, to predict a semantic fidelity score by learning from human annotations on dimensions of logical coherence, factual grounding, and instruction faithfulness. This critic will filter the augmented pool, and the resulting high-scoring data will be used to train a final LLM, with its performance benchmarked against baselines on held-out task suites.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, automated method for generating high-fidelity instruction-tuning data, directly addressing the data quality bottleneck in model scaling. It will yield a publicly available semantic critic model for data filtering and demonstrate that scaling with critic-curated data leads to more robust instruction-following models than scaling with raw synthetic data.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation and mixture weighting (DoReMi) exist, the integration of a learned, multi-dimensional semantic critic specifically for filtering instruction data is novel. This moves beyond simple perplexity or embedding-based filtering to actively safeguard the logical and factual integrity of the training corpus.

proposals.title: MINT: Meta-Instruction Tuning for Robust Generalization to Novel Instructions
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is a critical step for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but its mechanism is poorly understood. It remains unclear whether performance gains stem from improved instruction-following *generalization* or simply from learning the specific tasks in the tuning set. Disentangling these effects is essential for building models that robustly adapt to novel instructions, a core goal for general-purpose AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core benefit of instruction tuning is teaching a model to map from the *intent* expressed in an instruction to the appropriate task-solving procedure, a capability that can be meta-optimized separately from underlying task competence.
proposals.method: We will apply gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML) to a pre-trained LLM. The inner loop performs few-step instruction tuning on a subset of tasks. The outer loop meta-loss evaluates the tuned model on a held-out set of *novel instructions* for *seen* tasks, explicitly penalizing performance on the original training instructions to control for task knowledge. We will use a T5 or decoder-only architecture, with the meta-objective implemented via a contrastive loss between performance on novel vs. known instructions for the same task family.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, optimization-based test of the instruction-following hypothesis. It will yield a model, MINT, whose parameters are explicitly primed for instruction generalization, and a novel evaluation framework that separates instruction understanding from task knowledge.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for task adaptation, applying it to explicitly optimize for *instruction-following generalization* as a distinct capability from task learning is novel. This moves beyond scaling and data mixture optimization (DoReMi) to target the instruction interface itself.

proposals.title: Active Instruction Selection: Optimizing Data Mixtures for Efficient Instruction Tuning via Bayesian Optimization
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but current practice relies on large, static datasets (e.g., FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions) assembled heuristically. This is inefficient and fails to adapt to a model's specific knowledge gaps or target task distribution. Given the high cost of generating and training on instruction data, a principled, sample-efficient selection method is needed, analogous to the data mixture optimization in DoReMi but for the instruction space.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing instruction selection as an active learning problem over a learned performance landscape will allow for the construction of instruction-tuning datasets that are significantly more data-efficient than random or breadth-first selection, leading to better downstream task performance with fewer examples.
proposals.method: We will treat the space of possible instruction-response pairs as a structured input space. A surrogate model (e.g., a Gaussian Process or a small transformer) will be trained to predict a fine-tuning outcome metric (e.g., validation loss on a held-out task suite) from featurized representations of instruction batches. Starting from a small seed set, we will iteratively query a large, unlabeled pool of candidate instructions using acquisition functions like Expected Improvement or Predictive Entropy Search. The most informative batch will be selected, used for a short fine-tuning step on the target LLM, and the resulting performance will update the surrogate model, closing the loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, adaptive framework for instruction dataset curation that directly optimizes for final model performance. It will yield a practical algorithm for data-efficient instruction tuning and offer insights into the 'performance landscape' of instructions, identifying what makes an example informative for a given model state.
proposals.novelty: While active learning is established, its application to dynamically constructing instruction-tuning datasets by modeling a direct performance predictor is novel. This adapts concepts from Bayesian optimization and optimal sensor placement to the high-dimensional, semantic space of NLP instructions, moving beyond simple heuristics or static filtering.

proposals.title: Active Task Curriculum Learning: Discovering Core Instruction Sets for Efficient Model Tuning
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is crucial for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but current methods rely on large, static, and often redundant datasets (e.g., FLAN, Super-NaturalInstructions). This is inefficient and may not identify the minimal, most transferable core tasks needed for broad generalization. Given the scaling costs of training and the findings in DoReMi on data mixture optimization, a more principled, sample-efficient approach to defining the instruction-tuning curriculum is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-optimization process that actively discovers a minimal set of latent instruction tasks while selecting maximally informative examples for those tasks will yield a more sample-efficient and performant instruction-tuning pipeline than static dataset approaches.
proposals.method: We propose an iterative, transfer active learning framework. Starting with a small seed of labeled instructions and a large unlabeled pool, we use a task-aware acquisition function (e.g., based on gradient embeddings or predictive uncertainty) to query the pool. A clustering or mixture-of-experts model will group queried examples to infer latent task structures. The process alternates between refining the core task definitions (mixture weights) and selecting new examples that maximize expected utility across the evolving task set, inspired by data mixture optimization principles from DoReMi but applied at the task-discovery level.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for data-efficient instruction tuning, reducing the data and compute required for alignment. It will yield insights into the fundamental composition of instruction-following ability, identifying a compact 'task core' that drives generalization, contributing to the scaling laws for instruction data.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior active learning for fixed tasks or DoReMi's optimization of known domain mixtures, this method actively discovers and defines the latent task structure itself, jointly learning what to learn and what data to learn it from.

proposals.title: Generating the Frontier: A Meta-Controller for Dynamic Task Synthesis to Improve Language Model Robustness
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are trained on static, finite datasets, leading to brittle generalization on novel or compositionally complex instructions. Scaling data size alone, as shown in pre-2026 work, yields diminishing returns on robustness. This project addresses the core limitation of fixed task sets by proposing a system that learns to generate its own, unbounded curriculum, moving beyond memorization to true compositional understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: An LLM equipped with a meta-controller that models its own competency frontier and synthesizes novel, valid tasks just beyond that frontier will develop more robust and generalizable instruction-following capabilities than one trained on a static, pre-defined task set.
