proposals.method: We will implement a two-component system. First, a meta-controller, built as a smaller transformer, will be trained via reinforcement learning to generate task instructions. Its reward function will be based on the performance of a main, larger LLM (the "performer") on these generated tasks, encouraging tasks that are challenging but solvable. Second, the performer model will be continuously fine-tuned on this synthetically generated, dynamic curriculum. We will adapt techniques from DoReMi for data mixture optimization to balance synthetic and real data, and employ adversarial filtering to ensure task validity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for creating unbounded, adaptive training curricula, shifting the paradigm from data scaling to data synthesis for generalization. It will yield new benchmarks for evaluating compositional robustness and offer insights into the principles of task construction that models must internalize for true generalization.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior curriculum learning or data mixing strategies (e.g., DoReMi) that optimize over a fixed dataset, this method enables the model to generate entirely new, valid tasks, creating a self-improving loop focused on its own competency frontier. It directly tackles the limitation identified in pre-2026 work that LLMs cannot yet self-correct reasoning by enabling them to self-generate corrective training.

proposals.title: Core Competency Pruning: Identifying a Minimal Instruction Set for Robust Language Model Generalization
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning has become a dominant paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs), yet current practice relies on massive, ad-hoc collections of tasks, raising concerns about efficiency, redundancy, and the fundamental drivers of generalization. Inspired by the scaling principles in Wei Yu's prior work, this research asks if we can scale down the number of required training tasks while maintaining or even improving performance, moving beyond simple data mixture optimization (DoReMi) to a principled search for instructional primitives.
proposals.hypothesis: A minimal, conserved set of core instruction tasks, analogous to Hox gene regulatory modules in biology, exists and is sufficient to induce robust, general capabilities across a broad spectrum of downstream tasks and benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will start with a large, diverse instruction set (e.g., Flan or Super-NaturalInstructions) and a fixed-scale decoder-only LLM. We will employ an iterative, performance-guided pruning algorithm: in each round, we will ablate individual tasks or clusters and evaluate the resulting model on a comprehensive validation suite spanning reasoning, generation, and QA. Tasks whose removal does not degrade performance on any validation category will be pruned. This process will continue until a minimal, irreducible set of core tasks is identified. The final core set will be validated by training a model from scratch and comparing it to models trained on the full set and random subsets of equal size.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodology for distilling efficient, non-redundant instruction sets, reducing computational costs for alignment. It will offer empirical evidence for or against the existence of a 'toolkit' of fundamental instructional primitives, contributing to a more mechanistic understanding of how instruction tuning shapes model capabilities.
proposals.novelty: While prior work optimizes data mixtures for scaling laws, this proposal directly tests a biological analogy of core regulatory modules for task generalization, framing instruction pruning as a search for a minimal sufficient set of competencies rather than optimal weights.

proposals.title: TaskScaling: Identifying the Minimal Core Instruction Set for Effective Language Model Generalization
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is critical for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but current practice relies on massive, ad-hoc mixtures of tasks, leading to high computational cost and unclear contributions of individual data sources. Inspired by scaling laws for model size and data quantity, we lack a principled understanding of scaling down the number of distinct task types to find a minimal, effective core set that enables broad generalization, which would dramatically improve training efficiency and interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: A small, carefully curated set of fundamental instruction-following tasks, selected via data optimization principles, can yield generalization performance comparable to models trained on orders-of-magnitude larger, uncurated task collections.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework, originally for domain weighting, to optimize mixtures of instruction task types. Starting from a large corpus like Flan or Super-NaturalInstructions, we treat each task template as a 'domain'. A small proxy model will iteratively reweight task-type mixtures to minimize loss on a held-out validation set of diverse downstream benchmarks. The final, minimal core set will be used to train a larger target model (e.g., a T5 or LLaMA variant), whose performance will be compared against a baseline trained on the full task set across a broad evaluation suite covering reasoning, dialogue, and instruction following.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodology for distilling instruction-tuning datasets into a minimal core, reducing computational barriers for developing capable instruction-following models. It will yield insights into which task archetypes are essential for generalization, advancing the science of data curation beyond simple scaling.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi optimized domain mixtures, applying its paradigm to actively prune and identify a minimal sufficient set of instruction task *types* for generalization is novel. This inverts the prevailing 'scale-up' narrative by seeking a 'scale-down' law for task diversity.

proposals.title: Flux-Tuning: Efficient Instruction Tuning via Parameter Flux Analysis and Targeted Budget Allocation
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is critical for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but full fine-tuning is computationally prohibitive. Existing parameter-efficient methods (e.g., LoRA) apply updates uniformly across layers or modules, which may not be optimal. We need a principled, data-driven method to identify and update only the most critical parameters for instruction following under a strict compute budget, directly addressing scaling and efficiency challenges in model adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: The contribution of a pre-trained LLM's parameters to instruction-task generalization is non-uniform and can be quantified via a sensitivity-based 'flux' metric; concentrating a constrained parameter change budget on high-flux components yields superior performance per compute unit compared to uniform fine-tuning or standard parameter-efficient methods.
proposals.method: We first define 'instruction flux' for parameters using a gradient-based sensitivity analysis on a diverse instruction dataset, inspired by Metabolic Flux Analysis. We then formulate a constrained optimization problem where the total allowed change (measured via a norm constraint on parameter deltas) is allocated. We implement this via a sparsity-inducing, flux-weighted regularizer during tuning, or via a two-stage process: identifying high-flux sub-networks (e.g., specific attention heads or MLP blocks) and then applying dense updates exclusively to them. We will benchmark against full fine-tuning and LoRA on evaluation suites like BIG-Bench Hard and MMLU.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired framework for extreme parameter-efficient tuning, maximizing instruction-following gains under fixed budgets. It will yield a practical method for cost-effective model alignment and offer new insights into which internal components of LLMs are specialized for task generalization.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is adapting the systems biology concept of flux balance analysis to neural network tuning, moving beyond uniform or heuristic sparsity patterns to a data-driven, sensitivity-optimized allocation of a precise parameter change budget.

proposals.title: Curriculum Instruction Tuning: Joint Optimization of Task Mixture and Prompt Complexity
proposals.motivation: Current instruction tuning often uses static, uniformly sampled mixtures of tasks and instructions, ignoring the pedagogical principle of curriculum learning. Recent work like DoReMi shows the importance of optimizing data mixtures, but focuses on pretraining domains, not the complexity of instructions within tasks. Simultaneously, scaling laws highlight the efficiency of training trajectories, suggesting a need for a principled curriculum over instruction-following difficulty.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamically optimized curriculum that jointly schedules task types and the complexity (e.g., ambiguity, length, implicit reasoning steps) of their associated instructions will yield more sample-efficient instruction tuning and produce language models with superior robustness and generalization compared to static or uniformly complex mixtures.
proposals.method: We will extend the DoReMi framework to a two-dimensional optimization problem over (task, instruction-complexity) pairs. Using a small proxy model, we will estimate per-group learning progress via loss curvature or gradient norms to compute adaptive sampling weights. The curriculum will be implemented during large-scale instruction tuning (e.g., on a FLAN-style mixture), where the sampling distribution is updated periodically. We will evaluate on held-out tasks, complex instruction benchmarks (e.g., BBH), and robustness to ambiguous or lengthy prompts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, automated method for curriculum design in instruction tuning, moving beyond static task lists. It will yield empirical scaling laws for instruction complexity and demonstrate more efficient pathways to high-performance, robust instruction-following models.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi optimizes domain mixtures and prior curriculum learning work often sequences tasks, this proposal uniquely formulates and optimizes a joint curriculum over both task identity and the nuanced dimension of instruction complexity within each task.

proposals.title: StableCL: Stabilizing Contrastive Representation Learning with Data Mixture Optimization and Memory-Efficient Gradient Statistics
proposals.motivation: Contrastive learning for vision and language models, such as CLIP, requires extremely large batch sizes (e.g., 32k-65k) to achieve stable training and high performance, as they provide sufficient negative samples for the InfoNCE loss. This creates a prohibitive computational barrier for most researchers and limits exploration of data-efficient training regimes. Relaxing this batch size requirement is a critical scaling challenge for making state-of-the-art multimodal pretraining more accessible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically optimizing the data mixture (inspired by DoReMi) and employing a novel, memory-efficient method to maintain accurate gradient statistics across small batches, we can achieve the training stability and downstream performance of large-batch contrastive learning with drastically smaller per-GPU batch sizes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework to dynamically re-weight data domains in a multimodal stream (image-text pairs) to maximize the 'hardness' and diversity of in-batch negatives. Concurrently, we will develop a gradient statistics bank that aggregates and replays normalized feature embeddings from recent past batches, simulating a larger negative pool without the memory cost. This system will be integrated into a standard contrastive loss (e.g., a modified CLIP loss), and we will rigorously test stability and scaling laws on datasets like LAION, measuring zero-shot accuracy on downstream tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for training high-quality contrastive models without extreme batch sizes, democratizing access to this research area. It will also contribute new insights into the interplay between data mixture quality, gradient estimation, and stability in contrastive learning, extending scaling principles from pure language modeling to multimodal contexts.
proposals.novelty: While data mixture optimization and gradient averaging exist separately, their combined application to directly solve the large-batch dependency in contrastive learning is novel. The proposed gradient statistics bank is a non-obvious alternative to a static memory bank, designed for the streaming, large-scale data regime of modern pretraining.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for Multimodal Understanding: A Unified Study on Dense Prediction and Reasoning Tasks
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for model size, data, and compute have been pivotal in driving progress in unimodal language and vision models. However, their systematic application to multimodal tasks beyond zero-shot classification (e.g., visual question answering, referring expression comprehension, and dense captioning) remains underexplored. Understanding these scaling relationships is crucial for efficiently allocating resources to build the next generation of generalist multimodal agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combined scaling methodology—simultaneously scaling model parameters, curated multimodal dataset size, and computational budget—will reveal predictable power-law improvements in performance across a diverse suite of vision-language tasks, with the optimal data mixture (inspired by DoReMi) being task-dependent.
proposals.method: We will construct a scaling testbed using a unified encoder-decoder Transformer architecture, scaling from ~100M to ~10B parameters. We will pretrain on a dynamically optimized mixture of web-scale image-text data (using a DoReMi-inspired reweighting algorithm) and public multimodal datasets (e.g., LAION, COCO, Visual Genome). We will then conduct controlled scaling experiments, measuring performance on carefully selected benchmarks for visual grounding (RefCOCO), reasoning (VQA-v2, GQA), and generation (dense captioning), analyzing the scaling exponents and data mixture sensitivities for each task family.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of scaling laws for a broad spectrum of multimodal understanding tasks, yielding practical guidelines for scaling multimodal systems. It will also deliver a publicly available suite of scaled models and a diagnostic benchmark to assess where current scaling approaches succeed or fail, particularly for tasks requiring fine-grained reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling has been studied for image classification and language modeling, applying a combined scaling methodology with data mixture optimization to diverse, structured vision-language tasks is novel. The investigation into whether different task families obey distinct scaling laws or data requirements moves beyond the current focus on aggregate zero-shot performance.

proposals.title: NAS-CLIP: Neural Architecture Search for Optimal Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Contrastive learning frameworks like CLIP have become foundational for vision-language tasks, but their architecture design (e.g., transformer depth/width, projection head design) remains largely manual and heuristic. Given the high computational cost of scaling these models, a systematic, automated search for architectures optimized specifically for the contrastive objective is crucial for improving performance and efficiency. This directly addresses Adams Wei Yu's themes of scaling, performance, and tasks by moving beyond simple size scaling to intelligent architectural scaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a task-aware Neural Architecture Search (NAS), constrained by a contrastive loss objective, will discover non-trivial architectures that outperform manually designed baselines in terms of zero-shot accuracy and training efficiency for vision-language models.
proposals.method: We will employ a differentiable NAS (DARTS-like) search space encompassing key variables: the depth/width ratio of the visual encoder (ViT/ResNet variants) and text encoder, the design of the projection heads, and the temperature parameter. The search will be guided by a proxy task using a reduced dataset (e.g., a subset of LAION-400M) and optimized directly for the InfoNCE contrastive loss. The discovered architectures will then be scaled up and fully trained on the full dataset, with evaluation on standard zero-shot and linear probe benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a set of empirically optimal architectural guidelines for contrastive vision-language models, 2) an open-source NAS framework tailored for multimodal contrastive learning, and 3) new state-of-the-art model checkpoints that achieve higher accuracy or faster training than uniformly scaled counterparts, contributing to the efficient scaling paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While NAS is well-studied for supervised image classification, its application to the specific dual-encoder, contrastive objective of models like CLIP is novel. This moves beyond optimizing data mixtures (DoReMi) to co-optimizing the model architecture with the training objective, a less explored axis for scaling law improvement.

proposals.title: Task-Scaling Laws for Instruction Tuning: Achieving Generalization with Minimal Task Diversity
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is a critical step for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but current practice assumes that scaling the number of distinct tasks is essential for generalization. This leads to massive, often redundant, instruction datasets. Given the insights from DoReMi on data mixture optimization and scaling laws for pretraining, we must rigorously test if the same principles apply to the task diversity in instruction tuning.
proposals.hypothesis: Generalization in instruction-tuned models is governed by a scaling law in the complexity and compositional structure of a core task set, not merely by the raw count of distinct tasks. A minimal, algorithmically curated set of tasks, designed to maximize meta-learning and compositional generalization, can match or exceed the performance of models trained on massive, diverse task collections.
proposals.method: We will first define metrics for task diversity and complexity, building on prior work in meta-learning and task2vec. Using a DoReMi-inspired framework, we will treat the mixture of instruction-tuning tasks as an optimizable distribution. Starting from a large seed corpus (e.g., Flan or Super-NaturalInstructions), we will iteratively prune and synthesize tasks via LLM-based generation to create a minimal 'core' set that maximizes coverage of underlying skills and compositional operators. We will then train a series of models (e.g., T5, LLaMA) of varying sizes on this core set and on progressively larger, uncurated sets, evaluating zero-shot and few-shot performance on held-out benchmarks (MMLU, BIG-Bench Hard).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish empirical scaling laws for task diversity in instruction tuning, challenging the prevailing 'more tasks is better' heuristic. It will provide a principled, efficient methodology for curating high-impact instruction datasets, reducing computational cost and data redundancy. The findings will also inform theories of how LLMs acquire generalizable skills from limited task demonstrations.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are well-studied for model size and pretraining data, applying this lens to the diversity of *supervised tasks* in instruction tuning is novel. The approach directly challenges the implicit assumption in large-scale instruction collection efforts, proposing that optimal data mixture principles apply post-pretraining.

proposals.title: DoReMi for Instructions: Optimizing Instruction Template Mixtures for Efficient Language Model Alignment
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is critical for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but its performance is highly sensitive to the phrasing, style, and diversity of the instruction templates used. Current practice relies on handcrafted templates or simple heuristics, which is inefficient and suboptimal, limiting the accuracy and generalization of tuned models. Automating this process is a key scaling challenge for robustly adapting models to diverse downstream tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that instruction template creation can be formulated as a data mixture optimization problem, where an optimal distribution of instruction styles and phrasings for a given task set can be learned automatically, leading to more sample-efficient fine-tuning and superior final model performance compared to static template sets.
proposals.method: We adapt the DoReMi (Domain Reweighting with Minimax Optimization) framework, originally used for pretraining data mixtures, to the instruction tuning setting. We will train a small proxy model to reweight a large, diverse candidate pool of instruction templates (generated via LLM paraphrasing or mined from existing datasets) by minimizing the loss on a set of target tasks. The optimized mixture will then be used to instruction-tune a larger base model (e.g., a scaled LLaMA or T5 variant). We will evaluate the resulting model against baselines using static templates on held-out task accuracy and robustness benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated method for instruction template selection, reducing the need for manual engineering. It will demonstrate how data mixture optimization can be extended beyond pretraining to the alignment phase, offering a new lever for improving fine-tuning performance and efficiency. The resulting public code and optimized template mixtures will serve as a resource for the community.
proposals.novelty: While DoReMi has been applied to pretraining, applying its core optimization principle to the structured space of instruction templates for fine-tuning is novel. This approach moves beyond generating single instructions to optimizing distributions, directly linking instruction diversity to downstream task performance through a scalable algorithm.

proposals.title: Scaling Instruction-Tuning for Cross-Lingual Alignment: Data Mixture Optimization and Performance Analysis in Non-English Languages
proposals.motivation: While instruction tuning has unlocked strong zero-shot and reasoning capabilities in English LLMs, its application to non-English languages remains underexplored and faces significant data quality and quantity challenges. This creates a widening performance gap, limiting the global accessibility and utility of aligned AI assistants. Addressing this is critical for equitable AI development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically optimizing the data mixture for multilingual instruction tuning, informed by per-language model capabilities, will yield greater cross-lingual alignment and task performance gains than naive scaling or direct translation of English instruction sets.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework to optimize data mixtures for instruction tuning. Starting with a large-scale multilingual base model (e.g., a BLOOM or XGLM variant), we will create a proxy model to weight instruction datasets across multiple languages (e.g., from Bactrian-X, xP3). The core tuning will use standard supervised fine-tuning on the optimized mixture, with evaluation focusing on zero-shot task performance (using benchmarks like XNLI, XLSum, MGSM) and instruction-following quality across languages.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, scalable methodology for multilingual instruction alignment and deliver a performant, publicly available model. It will yield new insights into how instruction-tuning benefits transfer across languages with varying pretraining data scales, informing future data curation and model development efforts.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond simple translation or mixture-of-experts approaches by applying data mixture optimization—previously shown effective for pretraining—specifically to the instruction-tuning stage for multilingual models, a direction not yet fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: META-INSTRUCT: Meta-Learning for Few-Shot Adaptation in Instruction-Tuned Language Models
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning enables large language models (LLMs) to follow diverse tasks, but adaptation to new, unseen task families often requires extensive fine-tuning data. This limits the models' sample efficiency and agility in real-world applications where labeled examples are scarce. A method for rapid adaptation from minimal demonstrations is crucial for deploying performant models in specialized domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing multi-task instruction tuning as a meta-learning problem will yield a model initialization that can be adapted to novel instructions and task formats with significantly fewer gradient steps and examples than standard fine-tuning, without sacrificing performance.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework for the instruction-tuning setting. A base model (e.g., a pretrained LLM like LLaMA) will be meta-trained on a broad distribution of instruction-following tasks, where each 'task' is defined by a unique instruction template and associated dataset. The inner-loop optimization will simulate few-shot adaptation using a small support set for a given task, and the outer-loop will update the base initialization to minimize loss across a query set from the same task. We will experiment with first-order approximations and Reptile to manage computational cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, algorithmically grounded framework for improving the few-shot adaptability of instruction-tuned LLMs. It will yield quantitative benchmarks for fast task adaptation and could reduce the data requirements for specializing models to new domains, advancing towards more sample-efficient and flexible AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been explored in vision and narrow NLP domains, its systematic application to large-scale, multi-task instruction tuning for rapid adaptation to novel instructions is novel. This bridges the gap between the scalability of modern instruction tuning and the sample-efficient adaptation promises of meta-learning.

proposals.title: Contrastive Instruction Tuning: Improving Task Semantic Alignment and Robustness via Data Mixture Optimization
proposals.motivation: Instruction-tuned LLMs often exhibit brittleness to paraphrased or diverse formulations of the same task, indicating a misalignment between surface form and underlying task semantics. This limits robustness and effective scaling of instruction-following capabilities. Pre-2026 work on data mixture optimization (DoReMi) and scaling laws highlights the critical role of data quality and composition, but has not explicitly addressed this semantic alignment challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting standard instruction-tuning loss with a contrastive objective that pulls together latent representations of semantically equivalent instructions, while pushing apart those for distinct tasks, will improve model robustness to instruction phrasing and final task accuracy, especially for data-efficient scaling.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method building on the researcher's expertise. First, we adapt the DoReMi framework to use a proxy model to weight instruction clusters by their semantic diversity rather than domain. Second, during fine-tuning on this optimized mixture, we add a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on the final-layer [CLS] token or mean-pooled hidden states. Positive pairs will be different phrasings of the same task (e.g., from datasets like FLAN), while negatives will be instructions from different tasks. The combined loss will be L = L_SFT + λ * L_contrastive.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, scalable technique for improving instruction-following robustness, directly linking contrastive representation learning with data mixture optimization. It will yield empirical scaling laws for contrastive instruction tuning and demonstrate improved few-shot and zero-shot performance on held-out instruction formulations across benchmarks like MMLU and BIG-Bench Hard.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in vision and sentence embeddings, its integration into instruction tuning for LLMs to explicitly align task semantics is unexplored. The key novelty is combining this with a data-mixture optimization strategy (DoReMi) specifically geared towards maximizing semantic coverage of instructions.

proposals.title: ARCHITECT: Automated Retrieval of Compute-Heavy Instruction-Tuned Architectures via Neural Search
proposals.motivation: Instruction tuning is critical for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human intent, but current practice relies on fixed, dense architectures (e.g., full fine-tuning or standard adapters like LoRA) that may be suboptimal for the specific distribution of instruction-following tasks. As scaling laws highlight the importance of optimal allocation of compute, there is a need to move beyond one-size-fits-all architectural choices to discover more efficient, task-specialized structures.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural architecture search (NAS) framework, constrained by a fixed parameter or FLOP budget, can discover novel adapter architectures or sparse model variants that significantly outperform standard fine-tuning and hand-designed adapters on instruction-following benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop a weight-sharing NAS pipeline over a search space encompassing diverse adapter types (e.g., variants of LoRA, parallel adapters), attention head modifications, and selective activation of model components. The search objective will combine instruction-tuning loss on a mixture of tasks (inspired by DoReMi's data-centric view) with a hardware-aware cost penalty. We will employ a differentiable NAS controller (e.g., DARTS) to efficiently explore the space, initializing from a large pre-trained base model. The final architecture will be trained and evaluated on held-out instruction benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a systematic methodology for co-designing model architecture with instruction-tuning objectives, 2) novel, efficient architectural building blocks for alignment, and 3) empirical evidence on how optimal architectures for instruction following differ from those for pretraining or generic fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While NAS has been used for vision and language model pretraining, its application to the specific problem of instruction-tuning architecture design is unexplored. This moves beyond optimizing data mixtures (DoReMi) or scaling dense models to actively searching for a more efficient architectural inductive bias for instruction following.

proposals.title: TaskGen: Discovering and Characterizing Latent Tasks via Zero-Shot Prompting of Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current NLP research focuses on applying models to known tasks, but the ability to autonomously discover novel tasks from unstructured text remains unexplored. This is a critical step towards more general AI that can adapt to unforeseen challenges without human supervision. The scaling of zero-shot capabilities in large language models (LLMs) provides a new opportunity to invert this paradigm.
proposals.hypothesis: A sufficiently large, zero-shot capable language model can be prompted to systematically generate, validate, and characterize novel, coherent tasks from unstructured text corpora, creating a self-supervised task discovery pipeline.
proposals.method: We will use a large-scale decoder-only LLM (e.g., a scaled variant of the GPT-3/4 or PaLM architecture) as the core engine. The method involves a three-stage pipeline: 1) **Task Proposal**: The model is prompted to propose candidate task descriptions and input-output templates from diverse text snippets. 2) **Task Validation**: A self-consistency and discriminative prompting loop filters out incoherent or trivial tasks. 3) **Task Characterization**: The model clusters discovered tasks and infers metrics like difficulty and required skills, inspired by data mixture optimization principles from DoReMi. The scaling of model size will be explicitly studied as a key variable.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a formal framework and benchmark for automated task discovery, moving beyond static task lists. It will provide empirical evidence on how scaling and zero-shot ability relate to a model's capacity for task meta-cognition. The resulting discovered task taxonomy could inform future data curation and pretraining objectives.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs to perform zero-shot tasks, we invert the process to have the LLM define the tasks themselves. This differs from data mixture work (DoReMi) by focusing on task-space discovery rather than re-weighting known data domains, and from self-correction studies by targeting task generation rather than reasoning correction.

proposals.title: Dual-Tuning for Robustness: Instruction Un-Tuning to Mitigate Spurious Prompt Sensitivity in Large Language Models
proposals.motivation: Instruction-tuned LLMs are highly sensitive to prompt phrasing, often following misleading or adversarial instructions that degrade performance on core tasks. This fragility poses significant risks for reliable deployment. Current robustness methods, like adversarial training, often focus on specific attack patterns rather than teaching the model to intrinsically separate task intent from spurious instruction noise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-tuning procedure, which interleaves standard instruction-following fine-tuning with a novel 'instruction un-tuning' phase on corrupted prompts, can significantly improve a model's robustness by teaching it to ignore irrelevant or misleading instructional cues while preserving core task capabilities.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DoReMi framework for data mixture optimization to curate a 'robustness mixture' containing standard instruction-response pairs and corrupted pairs where task-relevant instructions are prefixed with irrelevant or contradictory commands. Using a base pretrained model (e.g., of the scale studied in 'Rethinking generative image pretraining'), we will implement a dual-phase training loop: Phase 1 performs standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on clean data; Phase 2 applies a gradient-ascent-like 'un-tuning' objective on the corrupted data to reduce the model's likelihood of following the spurious prefix, while using a conservation loss to anchor outputs to the correct task. We will scale experiments across model sizes to analyze robustness gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training algorithm for improving LLM robustness against prompt-based distribution shifts. It will yield new benchmarks for measuring instruction invariance and offer empirical scaling laws for robustness training, extending scaling research beyond accuracy to reliability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that adds adversarial examples to the training set, we formally frame robustness as an 'un-tuning' problem, explicitly reducing sensitivity to instructional noise. This approach directly connects data mixture optimization (DoReMi) with adversarial robustness, a link not deeply explored in pre-2026 literature.

ideas.title: Instruction tuning for multimodal models
ideas.description: Extend the instruction finetuning paradigm to vision-language or audio-language models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models

ideas.title: Synthesize a Dynamic, Compositional Task Curriculum for Robust Generalization
ideas.description: This approach abandons the search for a fixed, minimal task set. It employs a meta-controller that models the space of possible instructions and the model's own competency frontiers. This controller continuously generates novel, synthetically valid training tasks that are compositionally complex and lie just beyond current performance, creating an adaptive, unbounded curriculum. The system thus learns the principles of task construction and generalization, rather than memorizing a core set, leading to robust performance on unseen and compound instructions.

ideas.title: Apply scaling to other multimodal tasks
ideas.description: Extend the combined scaling methodology to other vision-language tasks beyond zero-shot image classification.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combined Scaling for Zero-shot Transfer Learning

ideas.title: Scale model and data further
ideas.description: Investigate performance limits by pretraining larger models on even larger-scale weakly supervised datasets.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Optimize model for even faster inference
ideas.description: Further refine the non-recurrent architecture for deployment in latency-critical applications.
ideas.sourceTitle: QANet: Combining Local Convolution with Global Self-Attention for Reading Comprehension

ideas.title: Adversarial Instruction Distillation for Dynamic, Compositional Generalization
ideas.description: This research challenges the premise that a minimal, static instruction set is sufficient for robust instruction tuning. Instead, we propose a method to dynamically grow a high-leverage instruction set through an adversarial distillation process. A generator model, trained on a small but evolving seed set, is continuously challenged by a critic that proposes failure modes—novel phrasings, task combinations, and edge cases. The system iteratively incorporates only those instructions that compel the generator to learn fundamental compositional skills, resulting in a compact yet highly generalizable training curriculum that provably covers a vast emergent space of user intents.

ideas.title: Modality-Aware Chain-of-Thought Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research investigates the conditional application of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data during instruction tuning of multimodal (vision-language, audio-language) models. It tests the hypothesis that CoT data, while beneficial for complex reasoning tasks, can degrade performance on tasks where the correct response is a direct perception or concise answer, especially when the primary signal comes from a non-textual modality. The core experiment involves creating a modality-annotated instruction tuning dataset and training models to learn when to engage in internal reasoning steps versus producing a succinct output, evaluating trade-offs on benchmarks spanning visual QA, audio captioning, and embodied instruction following.

ideas.title: Curriculum for Instruction Complexity: Optimizing the Trajectory of Task Difficulty
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that optimal training for language models involves not just mixing tasks uniformly or scaling instruction complexity in isolation, but jointly optimizing a curriculum over both task types and instruction complexity dimensions. The core hypothesis is that the learning trajectory should be governed by a schedule that strategically increases the complexity, ambiguity, or length of instructions for each task type at an optimal pace, which may differ across skills. This leads to a novel optimization problem: finding the weighted sampling distribution over (task, instruction-complexity) pairs that most efficiently drives the model toward robust, general-purpose instruction-following capability, potentially using measures of learning progress or loss curvature as signals.

ideas.title: Zero-shot evaluation is sufficient
ideas.description: Challenge that zero/one-shot performance fully captures model capabilities and robustness.
ideas.sourceTitle: GLaM: Efficient Scaling of Language Models with Mixture-of-Experts

ideas.title: Inverse: From zero-shot to task discovery
ideas.description: Use a zero-shot capable model to automatically identify and characterize novel tasks from unstructured text.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Instruction tuning requires massive task diversity
ideas.description: Challenge the need for many distinct tasks by achieving generalization with a minimal, curated task set.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Explore zero-shot capabilities in more depth
ideas.description: Systematically investigate the zero-shot reasoning and cross-modality transfer abilities of models like SimVLM.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Use meta-learning for fast task adaptation
ideas.description: Frame instruction tuning as a meta-learning problem to learn a parameter initialization for new tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Active Task Core Discovery for Efficient Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research proposes a method that uses transfer active learning not just for data selection within a fixed task set, but to actively discover and define the minimal core set of instruction tasks themselves. The process iteratively queries a large, unlabeled pool of potential instruction examples to identify which latent 'tasks' are most informative for improving broad generalization, constructing a compact, effective training curriculum. This results in a dual optimization: selecting the best examples for the currently identified core tasks, while also refining the very definition of the core task set needed for maximal downstream performance. The outcome is a highly sample-efficient instruction-tuning pipeline that learns both what to learn and which specific examples teach it most effectively.

ideas.title: Integrate neural architecture search
ideas.description: Use neural architecture search to find optimal model architectures for the contrastive learning task.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combined Scaling for Zero-shot Transfer Learning

ideas.title: Scale down: optimize mixtures for tiny models
ideas.description: Apply DoReMi to find optimal data mixtures for training extremely parameter-efficient models under severe compute constraints.
ideas.sourceTitle: DoReMi: Optimizing Data Mixtures Speeds Up Language Model Pretraining

ideas.title: Meta-Optimizing for Instruction Following Generalization
ideas.description: This research direction uses gradient-based meta-learning to explicitly optimize a model's parameters such that, after instruction tuning, it shows maximal improvement on its ability to follow novel instructions for held-out tasks, while controlling for prior task knowledge. The meta-objective is designed to separate gains in instruction-following generalization from gains in underlying task competence. This provides a direct, optimization-based test of the hypothesis that instruction tuning works primarily by teaching models how to follow instructions, and it yields a model whose core capability is robustly adapting to the intent of new instructions, not just performing known tasks better.

ideas.title: Improve efficiency of prefix language modeling
ideas.description: Develop more efficient architectures or training techniques for the prefix language modeling objective in VLP.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Scale down the number of training tasks
ideas.description: Find the minimal effective set of core instruction tasks for broad generalization.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models

ideas.title: The Over-Explanation Penalty: When Chain-of-Thought Training Impedes Direct Response Generation
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data is universally beneficial by investigating its potential to harm performance on tasks requiring concise or direct answers. Drawing an analogy from motor learning in sports science—where over-focusing on the step-by-step mechanics of an automated skill can cause 'choking' and degrade performance—we hypothesize that exclusive fine-tuning on detailed CoT data may similarly 'over-train' an LLM's analytical reasoning mode, making it prone to 'computational choking' when a direct answer is required. The adapted solution involves creating a differential training paradigm. Instead of training only on CoT data, models would be trained on a mixed dataset containing both CoT examples and direct answer examples, with explicit contextual cues distinguishing the two. The core investigation would be whether such training enables the model to learn a 'mode-switching' capability, using cues in the prompt or task context to generate either detailed reasoning or concise answers appropriately, thereby eliminating the potential performance penalty of CoT-only training on direct response tasks.

ideas.title: Identify a Minimal Instruction Set via Core Competency Pruning
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Evolutionary Developmental Biology, where a minimal set of core genetic pathways (like Hox genes) governs vast morphological diversity, this idea seeks to find a minimal effective set of core instruction tasks for broad generalization in language models. The analogous solution involved comparative mapping across species to isolate essential, reusable regulatory modules. Adapted to machine learning, this research would systematically prune tasks from a large, diverse instruction training set, using performance on a broad validation suite to identify a small, conserved 'core competency set' of fundamental task types. The goal is to discover if such a minimal instructional 'toolkit' exists and is sufficient to induce robust, general capabilities, thereby scaling down the number of required training tasks while maintaining performance.

ideas.title: Semantic-Fidelity-Guided Augmentation for Instruction Data Generation
ideas.description: This approach augments instruction data using a two-stage, critic-guided pipeline. First, a suite of augmentation operators (e.g., counterfactual rewriting, constraint variation, style transfer) generates candidate instruction-output pairs. Second, a separately trained semantic fidelity critic model evaluates each candidate for logical coherence, factual grounding, and task alignment, filtering out low-quality or nonsensical examples. This ensures the synthetic data maintains high instructional integrity while expanding diversity, leading to more robust model training without poisoning from flawed synthetic examples.

ideas.title: Use neural architecture search for tuning
ideas.description: Automatically search for optimal model architectures or adapters specifically tailored for instruction following.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Inverse problem: generate images from text
ideas.description: Use the same architecture and pretraining but invert the task to generate images from textual prefixes.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Scale up with massive synthetic data
ideas.description: Use the model's speed to train on orders of magnitude more machine-generated data.
ideas.sourceTitle: QANet: Combining Local Convolution with Global Self-Attention for Reading Comprehension

ideas.title: Combine instruction tuning with parameter-efficient methods
ideas.description: Investigate instruction finetuning using adapters or LoRA for more efficient large-scale task adaptation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models

ideas.title: Efficient Instruction Tuning via Task Ablation and Compute-Aware Curriculum Learning
ideas.description: This research direction seeks to find the Pareto-optimal frontier between instruction set size and compute allocation for instruction tuning under a hard total compute budget. Instead of using thousands of tasks or a static small set, we propose a joint optimization: first, algorithmically ablate a massive seed task set to identify a minimal, high-leverage core that maximizes latent skill coverage. Second, within the fixed parameter update budget, we dynamically allocate compute via a curriculum, intensively training on this core set before fine-tuning allocation on broader but sparser tasks. This synergy enables the discovery of a computationally optimal 'instruction genome' and a training schedule that maximizes generalizability per FLOP.

ideas.title: Decomposing and Quantifying the Causal Impact of Instruction Data Attributes on Model Capabilities
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the vague concept of 'quality' by decomposing it into measurable, orthogonal data attributes. We develop a framework for generating synthetic instruction-tuning datasets where attributes like instruction clarity, reasoning depth, and factual grounding are systematically manipulated. By training models on these controlled datasets and evaluating performance on held-out benchmarks, we can isolate the causal impact of each data attribute on distinct model capabilities. This provides actionable, evidence-based guidelines for data curation that are directly tied to desired model behaviors.

ideas.title: Dual: Instruction un-tuning for robustness
ideas.description: Finetune models to ignore spurious instructions, improving robustness against adversarial or misleading prompts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Modality-Balanced Instruction Tuning with Adversarial Counterfactuals
ideas.description: This research addresses the risk of modality collapse in multimodal instruction tuning by designing a training framework that enforces balanced cross-modal understanding. It generates and incorporates adversarial counterfactual examples where relying on a single modality leads to incorrect responses, penalizing such bias during training. The system uses a combination of gradient-based adversarial attacks on input modalities and a specialized loss function to measure and minimize modality reliance disparities. This results in a robust multimodal model that genuinely integrates visual or audio information with language to follow complex, grounded instructions.

ideas.title: Explore MoE for multimodal tasks
ideas.description: Extend the sparsely activated mixture-of-experts architecture to vision-language and other multimodal learning problems.
ideas.sourceTitle: GLaM: Efficient Scaling of Language Models with Mixture-of-Experts

ideas.title: Apply neural architecture search
ideas.description: Automate the discovery of optimal convolution and attention block configurations for QA.
ideas.sourceTitle: QANet: Combining Local Convolution with Global Self-Attention for Reading Comprehension

ideas.title: Compositional Mixture-of-Experts PEFT for Robust Multi-Task Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework designed to overcome catastrophic interference in multi-task instruction tuning. The system replaces a single LoRA or adapter module with a dynamic, sparse mixture of experts, where each expert is a small, task-specialized PEFT module, and a lightweight router network selects and blends them per instruction. To ensure stable training and robust generalization, the method incorporates a spectral regularization loss to encourage orthogonality between different experts' parameter subspaces and a strategic data rehearsal schedule. This approach enables efficient, large-scale adaptation to diverse instructions while preserving model performance and facilitating compositional skill reuse.

ideas.title: Minimal Effective Task Curriculum with Task-Specific Reasoning Formats
ideas.description: This research investigates how to construct a minimal, effective instruction-tuning dataset by jointly optimizing for both task diversity and task-specific reasoning format. The core hypothesis is that for a compact set of core tasks, generalization is maximized not just by selecting the right tasks, but by using the correct reasoning style (Chain-of-Thought or direct) for each. The study will systematically measure how CoT data can harm performance on inherently concise tasks within a minimal curriculum, and use these findings to design a small-scale training regimen that strategically applies or withholds CoT to build a model proficient across a broad range of reasoning demands without inefficiency or interference.

ideas.title: Scale sequence length for long-form VLM
ideas.description: Train SimVLM on very long image-text sequences for tasks like dense captioning or long document understanding.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Instruction tuning for non-English languages
ideas.description: Apply the instruction-tuning method to language models pretrained on multilingual or non-English corpora.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Robust Multi-Domain Instruction Tuning with Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation
ideas.description: This research develops a training methodology for specialized domain models that preserves general capabilities and safety. It uses a dynamic data scheduler that blends domain-specific instructions with a sustained stream of general-purpose tasks and safety benchmarks. The training is guided by a multi-faceted evaluation suite monitoring for catastrophic forgetting, ensuring the final model is both an expert in its domain and a robust, aligned assistant. This approach yields specialists that retain the versatility and safety of their base models.

ideas.title: Disentangling Procedural from Declarative Gains in Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that instruction tuning improves a model's underlying task knowledge. Drawing an analogy from cognitive psychology's method of disentangling procedural from declarative knowledge, we propose a 'format transfer' experiment. The analogous solution involves testing learning transfer when the procedural context changes but the core knowledge remains constant. Adapted to LLMs, we would instruction-tune a model on a corpus of tasks using a consistent instructional format (Format A). Evaluation would then pit performance on the same core tasks presented in a novel, unseen format (Format B) against performance on entirely novel tasks. A strong dissociation—significant gains on the novel-format tasks but not on the novel-content tasks—would provide evidence that the primary benefit of instruction tuning is the acquisition of a generalized 'procedure' for interpreting and executing instructions, rather than the transfer of specific, declarative task-solving knowledge.

ideas.title: Challenge necessity of any image-text alignment
ideas.description: Train a model with a single objective on completely unaligned image and text streams.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Instruction Tuning via Foundational Skill Primitives
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that instruction tuning requires massive task diversity by drawing an analogy from motor control in robotics. In that field, complex behaviors are solved not by training on every task, but by identifying and mastering a small set of reusable motor primitives (e.g., reach, grasp) that can be recombined. Adapted to NLP, the research would seek to identify a minimal set of foundational 'cognitive skill primitives' (such as reformulation, comparison, and conditional reasoning) and intensively instruction-tune a model on them. The core hypothesis is that achieving robust mastery of these high-leverage primitives will enable combinatorial generalization to a vast space of complex instructions, potentially making the curation of thousands of disparate tasks unnecessary.

ideas.title: Adopt mixture-of-experts for scaling
ideas.description: Use sparse mixture-of-experts models to scale parameters efficiently for the prefix LM VLP task.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Relax the need for extreme batch sizes
ideas.description: Develop training methods that achieve stable contrastive learning without requiring batches of 65536.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combined Scaling for Zero-shot Transfer Learning

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Placement for Instruction Tuning via Active Learning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to optimal sensor placement from environmental monitoring to the problem of selecting instruction examples for fine-tuning. In the analogous field, a predictive model (like a Gaussian Process) of a spatial phenomenon is built, and active learning criteria select the most informative geographic points for new sensor deployment. Adapted here, we frame the space of instruction examples as a complex performance landscape. We train a surrogate model to predict fine-tuning outcomes (e.g., validation accuracy on target tasks) from features of instruction-response pairs. Using active learning acquisition functions (e.g., entropy search, expected improvement), we iteratively query for the construction of new instruction examples in regions where the surrogate model is most uncertain or predicts the highest potential performance boost. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive system for selecting maximally informative data for instruction tuning, dramatically improving data efficiency over random or heuristic selection.

ideas.title: Multi-task across many QA datasets
ideas.description: Leverage training speed to jointly train on all available QA benchmarks simultaneously.
ideas.sourceTitle: QANet: Combining Local Convolution with Global Self-Attention for Reading Comprehension

ideas.title: Instruction Tuning via Metabolic Flux Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of Metabolic Flux Analysis from Systems Biology to the problem of instruction tuning under a strict compute/parameter change budget. In metabolic engineering, a fixed nutrient budget is optimally allocated to high-yield pathways while suppressing inefficient ones. Analogously, we propose a method to perform a sensitivity analysis on a pre-trained language model to map its 'instruction flux': identifying which parameters (or layers, attention heads) contribute most to learning and generalizing from instruction data. The strict training compute constraint is then enforced by applying sparsity-inducing optimization or targeted gradient updates, concentrating the entire allowed parameter change budget on these high-flux components. This creates a maximally efficient tuning process that optimizes performance per unit of update, rather than uniformly fine-tuning the entire network.

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Instruction Tuning via Multi-Sensory Alignment Protocols
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience's sensory substitution training, this idea proposes a structured protocol for instruction tuning multimodal models. The analogous solution uses known sensory modalities to anchor and align novel input signals through paired, task-oriented training. Adapted here, the research would generate instruction-following datasets where each semantic task (e.g., description, reasoning, manipulation) is presented with aligned inputs from vision, audio, and text modalities. The model is tuned not just to follow instructions per modality, but to build a unified representation by explicitly connecting the modalities through their shared instructional and semantic goals, thereby improving cross-modal generalization and coherence in responses.

ideas.title: Incorporate contrastive learning for instructions
ideas.description: Use contrastive loss to better align diverse instruction formulations with their intended task semantics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Dynamic Online Task Sampling via Learning Progress Signals
ideas.description: This research challenges static mixing strategies by proposing a system that dynamically optimizes task sampling during training based on real-time learning progress signals. Instead of a fixed curriculum or pre-computed weights, the method continuously estimates which tasks offer the highest potential for loss reduction given the model's current parameters. This adaptive approach aims to maximize overall learning efficiency by allocating more samples to tasks in their 'learning sweet spot,' while mitigating forgetting by periodically revisiting mastered tasks. The core hypothesis is that such online, model-aware optimization will outperform any static mixing strategy, including uniform sampling, across a wide range of multi-task and continual learning benchmarks.

ideas.title: Automate or optimize instruction template creation
ideas.description: Develop methods to generate or select optimal natural language instructions for finetuning tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Dynamic Instruction Data Pruning via Targeted Forgetting
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel, closed-loop approach to studying instruction data quality by integrating targeted forgetting techniques. Instead of just scaling data, we systematically apply instruction un-tuning methods to a model trained on a large, mixed-quality dataset to see which subsets of instructions are 'forgettable' versus 'persistent.' The persistence of a learned behavior after targeted un-tuning becomes a direct, operational metric for the quality and impact of that specific instruction data. This allows for the iterative pruning of low-quality data and the identification of a minimal, high-quality instruction set, turning the forgetting process into an active tool for data curation and quality assessment.

ideas.title: Incorporate retrieval-augmented generation
ideas.description: Augment the prefix LM model with a non-parametric memory for retrieving relevant knowledge during generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Incorporate gating mechanisms from RNNs
ideas.description: Add gated linear units or similar mechanisms to the convolution layers for better information flow.
ideas.sourceTitle: QANet: Combining Local Convolution with Global Self-Attention for Reading Comprehension

ideas.title: Closed-Loop, Model-Guided Curriculum Synthesis for Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This approach automates curriculum learning by creating a feedback loop between the model's state and the training data generator. During instruction tuning, the system uses probes and performance metrics to diagnose the model's specific competency gaps. It then synthesizes or retrieves instruction-response pairs that are precisely calibrated to be at the edge of the model's current capabilities, dynamically adjusting complexity and domain focus. This results in a personalized, efficient curriculum that directly targets emergent weaknesses rather than following a fixed human developmental template.

ideas.title: Compute-Optimal Instruction Tuning via Scaling-Aware Allocation
ideas.description: This research optimizes instruction tuning by treating total training FLOPs as the strict constraint, not just parameters. It uses established neural scaling laws to formulate a joint optimization problem for allocating the compute budget between model size and the number of instruction-tuning examples. The system dynamically determines the optimal parameter count and dataset size for a given FLOPs budget, ensuring the model is trained at the compute frontier for maximum instruction-following capability. This approach guarantees Pareto-optimal performance under the compute constraint, moving beyond the naive fixed-parameter assumption.

ideas.title: Scaffolded Skill Acquisition Before Instruction Tuning for Extremely Small Models
ideas.description: This research investigates the minimum viable model size for robust instruction-following by first establishing a necessary foundation of procedural reasoning. Instead of applying instruction tuning directly, the approach uses a two-phase training regimen: first, the small model is pre-trained on a curriculum of explicit reasoning traces and task decomposition examples to build internal compositional skills. Second, it undergoes instruction tuning on a broad set of prompts. The evaluation rigorously measures generalization to novel, complex instructions that require combining learned skills, ensuring any performance gains reflect true instruction understanding rather than shallow pattern matching.

ideas.title: Adversarially Robust and Critic-Guided Knowledge Distillation for Safe Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research proposes a knowledge distillation framework designed to produce smaller, efficient models that are more robust and aligned than their large teacher models. The system integrates a critic model that actively screens and filters the teacher's instruction-response pairs for biases, factual inaccuracies, and safety violations before they are used for distillation. Furthermore, the student model is trained with an adversarial objective, where it learns to resist generating the problematic patterns identified in the teacher's behavior. This results in a compact model that captures the teacher's general capability and instruction-following skill while explicitly mitigating its documented failure modes.

ideas.title: The Double-Edged Chain: A Multi-Dimensional Taxonomy and Framework for Evaluating Harmful Behavioral Shifts Induced by Chain-of-Thought Data
ideas.description: This research systematically investigates the nuanced ways in which Chain-of-Thought (CoT) training data can induce detrimental behavioral shifts in language models, moving beyond a simplistic view of performance harm. We develop a taxonomy of potential harms, including instruction ignoring, reasoning leakage in concise tasks, and efficiency degradation. Using a controlled data generation and model training pipeline, we measure impacts across multiple dimensions: accuracy, conciseness, instruction fidelity, and inference cost. The outcome is a diagnostic framework to guide the safe and effective use of CoT data by predicting and mitigating its negative side effects.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Niche Modeling Data Augmentation to Instruction Generation
ideas.description: This idea adapts data augmentation techniques from Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) to the problem of creating diverse instruction examples for language model training. In ENM, synthetic species occurrence data is generated by defining a multidimensional environmental niche (using variables like temperature and precipitation) and creating new plausible data points within that space via expansion, perturbation, and interpolation. The adapted solution applies this core principle to instruction generation by first defining a 'conceptual instruction niche' using feature vectors that capture an instruction's semantics, syntax, intent, and complexity. Synthetic instructions are then generated by performing analogous operations—such as semantic variable interpolation, intent-preserving syntactic perturbation, and complexity scaling—within this defined feature space. This method provides a principled, domain-aware approach to data augmentation that moves beyond simple lexical substitution, systematically expanding instruction diversity while preserving functional validity and task alignment, much like ecologists generate plausible species occurrences within suitable environmental bounds.

ideas.title: Scale up supervision for correction training
ideas.description: Train models on massive datasets of flawed reasoning paired with correction processes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Large Language Models Cannot Self-Correct Reasoning Yet

ideas.title: Bias-Aware Knowledge Distillation for Debiased Instruction-Tuned Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel bias-aware knowledge distillation framework. It first analyzes how specific instruction-tuning data and techniques amplify or mitigate biases in a large teacher model. This analysis then directly informs the distillation loss function and data selection, prioritizing the transfer of knowledge that leads to helpful, harmless, and unbiased outputs. The outcome is a smaller, deployable model that is not just a compressed version but a consciously debiased one, achieving efficiency with improved alignment.

ideas.title: Scale down for edge device deployment
ideas.description: Create highly efficient, small-scale versions of the BASIC model for on-device zero-shot learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combined Scaling for Zero-shot Transfer Learning

ideas.title: Apply DoReMi to multimodal pretraining data mixtures
ideas.description: Extend the DoReMi method to optimize mixture proportions across image, text, and video domains for multimodal foundation models.
ideas.sourceTitle: DoReMi: Optimizing Data Mixtures Speeds Up Language Model Pretraining

ideas.title: Apply OLM to Recurrent Neural Networks
ideas.description: Extend the orthogonal linear module to RNNs for more stable hidden state dynamics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Orthogonal Weight Normalization: Solution to Optimization over Multiple Dependent Stiefel Manifolds in Deep Neural Networks

ideas.title: Dense models are always inefficient
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that dense scaling is inherently worse than sparse MoE for all tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: GLaM: Efficient Scaling of Language Models with Mixture-of-Experts

ideas.title: Apply mixture-of-experts to contrastive learning
ideas.description: Use mixture-of-experts architectures to efficiently scale model capacity beyond dense 3B parameter models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Combined Scaling for Zero-shot Transfer Learning

ideas.title: Instruction Tuning for Specialized Domains via Curriculum Learning from Expert Demonstrations
ideas.description: The original idea proposes applying instruction fine-tuning to models specialized for domains like code, science, or medicine. Drawing an analogy from Cognitive Science and Skill Acquisition, where complex skills are taught using expert demonstrations and a scaffolded curriculum (part-task training leading to integrated performance), this expanded idea adapts that solution. The adapted research idea is to implement curriculum learning for instruction tuning in specialized domains. This involves structuring the fine-tuning data into a progressive sequence, starting with high-quality expert demonstrations (e.g., textbook solutions), moving to decomposed sub-problems, and finally advancing to complex, open-ended instructions. This methodology, inspired by pedagogical best practices, aims to train more robust, reliable, and instruction-following models for technical domains by mimicking a structured learning pathway.

ideas.title: Explore mixture proportions of finetuning tasks
ideas.description: Systematically study how the mixture ratio of different task types affects final zero-shot performance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Apply curriculum learning to instruction order
ideas.description: Structure the sequence of instruction-tuning tasks from simple to complex to improve learning efficiency.
ideas.sourceTitle: Finetuned Language Models Are Zero-Shot Learners

ideas.title: Verifiable Task Forgetting with Latent Space Intervention and Robustness Audits
ideas.description: This research develops methods for post-hoc instruction removal that are verifiably robust, moving beyond superficial output suppression. It designs un-tuning algorithms that target and disrupt the latent feature representations associated with a specific instructed task or behavior. The core innovation is a coupled verification framework that rigorously audits the success of forgetting using metrics like re-learning speed, adversarial recoverability, and generalization stability, ensuring the effect is genuine and not easily circumvented.

ideas.title: Scale model size beyond trillion parameters
ideas.description: Push the mixture-of-experts architecture to even larger parameter counts, like tens of trillions.
ideas.sourceTitle: GLaM: Efficient Scaling of Language Models with Mixture-of-Experts

ideas.title: Apply minimalist framework to other modalities
ideas.description: Extend the simple, single-objective pretraining paradigm to integrate audio or video with language.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Apply data augmentation from computer vision
ideas.description: Use synthetic data generation and augmentation techniques from CV to create diverse instruction examples.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models

ideas.title: Use contrastive learning from CLIP
ideas.description: Combine the prefix LM loss with a contrastive image-text alignment loss from models like CLIP.
ideas.sourceTitle: SimVLM: Simple Visual Language Model Pretraining with Weak Supervision

ideas.title: Explore advanced data augmentation methods
ideas.description: Investigate other data generation techniques beyond backtranslation to further improve model performance.
ideas.sourceTitle: QANet: Combining Local Convolution with Global Self-Attention for Reading Comprehension

ideas.title: Instruction Tuning for Extremely Small Models via Critical Period Stimulation
ideas.description: This research explores the lower bound of model size where instruction tuning provides meaningful gains by drawing an analogy from developmental neurobiology. The analogous problem is how to achieve robust functional development with a limited number of neurons, solved by identifying and exploiting 'critical periods' with targeted sensory stimulation. The adapted idea investigates whether extremely small language models (e.g., <10M parameters) have a 'critical period' early in fine-tuning where exposure to a carefully designed curriculum of foundational instructional tasks—such as decomposed reasoning steps, intent clarification, and output formatting—can most efficiently wire in general instruction-following capability. The core hypothesis is that this targeted, phase-specific 'stimulation' will yield disproportionate gains compared to standard instruction tuning applied later or with less curated data, effectively lowering the viable size bound for usefully tunable models.

ideas.title: Transfer active learning for data selection
ideas.description: Use active learning to select the most informative instruction examples for finetuning.
ideas.sourceTitle: Scaling Instruction-Finetuned Language Models

ideas.title: Compute-Constrained Knowledge Distillation for Pareto-Optimal Instruction Tuning
ideas.description: This research investigates how to optimally allocate a strict, fixed compute budget between two competing processes: directly instruction-tuning a model and using a portion of that budget to first train and then distill knowledge from a larger, more capable teacher model. The core challenge is to formulate and solve for the optimal split—determining how much compute to invest in creating a superior teacher via scaling versus using that compute for direct student training or distillation steps. This leads to a systematic methodology for identifying the most compute-efficient pathway to achieve a target performance level for a model of a given size, moving beyond static distillation recipes. The outcome is a decision framework that reveals the Pareto-optimal frontier of performance for instruction-tuned models under any given compute constraint.

ideas.title: Domain-Adaptive Instruction Tuning with Task-Aware Chain-of-Thought Control
ideas.description: This research investigates how to optimally integrate Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data during instruction tuning for specialized domains like medicine, law, or code. It moves beyond the binary question of whether CoT helps or harms, instead developing techniques to teach models to recognize which domain-specific tasks require explicit, step-by-step reasoning and which demand concise, direct answers as per professional norms. The outcome is a single model capable of adaptive reasoning styles, whose instruction-tuning process includes meta-annotations or learned triggers to switch reasoning modes, optimizing both accuracy and output appropriateness for the domain.

ideas.title: Apply Teacher Assistant Networks for Multi-Stage Instruction Distillation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from pedagogy, where master teachers use assistants to scaffold learning for novices, this idea adapts the 'teacher assistant' concept to neural network distillation. The core method involves a multi-stage distillation pipeline for instruction tuning. Instead of a single, large performance gap between teacher and student, a series of intermediate-sized 'teacher assistant' models are sequentially distilled. The largest, instruction-tuned teacher model distills into a moderately sized assistant. This assistant, now possessing a refined and more tractable version of the instructional knowledge, subsequently acts as the teacher for a smaller model, and so on. This progressive, scaffolded approach aims to preserve the nuanced instruction-following behavior more effectively than direct distillation, leading to a smaller, more efficient final model with higher fidelity to the original teacher's capabilities.

ideas.title: Progressive Complexity Scaffolding via Cognitive Load Theory
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to managing cognitive load from Educational Psychology to the scaling of instruction-following in AI. The analogous field's solution—progressive complexity scaffolding from Cognitive Load Theory—is applied by structuring an instruction-tuning dataset as a curriculum. The core adaptation is to systematically increase the complexity and length of instructions along defined axes (e.g., reasoning depth, constraint interplay, contextual ambiguity) rather than merely increasing the count of tasks. This approach aims to build an AI's ability to handle intricate, composite commands by gradually integrating sub-skills into more sophisticated problem-solving schemas, mirroring how human expertise is developed through carefully sequenced learning challenges.
