ideas.title: Optimal Curriculum via Simulated Annealing of Task Distributions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that uniform mixing of all tasks is optimal for multi-task or continual learning. It draws an analogy from metallurgy, where simulated annealing finds a material's optimal low-defect structure by carefully controlling the cooling process. The analogous solution is to apply a simulated annealing algorithm to the problem of task sampling. The training process begins with a high 'temperature,' permitting aggressive exploration of skewed, non-uniform task distributions. The 'energy' is a function of the learner's performance (e.g., loss, transfer metrics). The temperature schedule is gradually reduced, guiding the search toward a final, optimized task probability distribution. This method adapts the physical principle of annealing to automatically discover curriculum-like or weighted sampling strategies that may yield better generalization and stability than a static uniform mix.

ideas.title: Human-Aligned Complexity Scaling via Cognitive Load and Preference Feedback
ideas.description: This research scales instruction complexity in a human-aligned manner by first defining complexity through measurable cognitive load dimensions like reasoning depth and ambiguity. A generative system produces instructions across this spectrum, but their utility is continuously evaluated by a human preference model trained on feedback regarding plausibility and task value. The final training curriculum is dynamically adjusted to prioritize complex instructions that humans rate as highly desirable and realistic, ensuring the model's capability gains translate directly to improved performance on genuine, challenging user requests.

ideas.title: Optimal Model Size for Instruction Tuning: A Diminishing Returns Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that scaling model size always improves instruction tuning by drawing an analogy to pharmacology's dose-response principle. In drug development, increasing dosage yields diminishing returns and eventual toxicity, leading to the identification of an optimal dose. Adapted to AI, we treat model size (parameters) as the 'dose' and instruction-tuning performance as the 'therapeutic response.' Given a fixed compute budget for tuning, the research would empirically construct a 'tuning response curve' across a range of model sizes to identify if and where performance plateaus or even degrades, thereby determining an optimal model size for efficient instruction tuning.

ideas.title: Selective Neural Pruning for Task De-biasing
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from immunology—specifically, the controlled suppression of hyperspecific immune memory in allergy desensitization—to the problem of post-hoc instruction un-tuning. The analogous solution involves targeted exposure combined with regulatory signals to dampen specific pathways. The adapted idea is to develop 'selective neural pruning for task de-biasing': a method that identifies the neural circuitry (e.g., specific attention heads or feed-forward layers) most responsible for a model's adherence to an unwanted instructed task. The model is then given controlled re-exposure to that task paired with a regularization or opposing loss signal, encouraging it to prune or significantly dampen the activity of only those specific pathways. This aims to surgically 'forget' the instructed behavior while minimizing damage to the model's general capabilities and performance on other desired tasks, addressing the scaling dual of adding and removing specific functionalities.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Instruction Parsing from Latent Knowledge Transfer in Tuned Models
ideas.description: This research employs a causal mediation analysis framework to rigorously separate the effects of instruction tuning. It uses a carefully constructed dataset of 'instruction-twin' tasks, where core knowledge is held constant but instructional surface forms are radically altered. By performing targeted model interventions (e.g., activation patching) during evaluation on these twins, the design traces whether performance gains on novel instructions flow through pathways associated with instruction parsing or through pathways containing pre-existing task knowledge. This allows for a definitive, causal claim about the primary mechanism of improvement.

ideas.title: Bias-Specific Instruction Un-Tuning: Targeted Mitigation of Social Biases and Toxicity
ideas.description: This research direction investigates methods to selectively 'un-tune' or forget specific social biases and toxic associations that are amplified or introduced during large-scale instruction finetuning. It moves beyond merely measuring these biases to developing post-hoc interventions that can mitigate them, treating harmful bias patterns as undesirable 'task' effects to be removed. The core challenge is to design un-tuning algorithms that target and degrade these specific undesirable associations while preserving the model's general instruction-following performance and knowledge. Success would provide a crucial safety tool for refining deployed models and studying the causal relationship between instruction data and emergent biases.

ideas.title: Causal Mediation Analysis of Dataset Properties on Bias Emergence in Instruction-Tuned LLMs
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlation to establish causality by constructing a controlled intervention framework. It creates multiple, parallel instruction-tuning datasets from a common seed corpus, systematically varying single properties like stylistic framing, demographic distribution in examples, or implicit value judgments. Identical base models are then fine-tuned on these dataset variants, and their outputs are evaluated using a comprehensive, multi-metric bias audit suite. The design isolates which specific attributes of instruction-tuning data causally mediate increases or decreases in specific social biases and toxicity, providing actionable levers for safer model alignment.

ideas.title: Use a Spaced Repetition Curriculum from Cognitive Science
ideas.description: This idea adapts the cognitive science solution of spaced repetition systems (SRS) to the design of a psychologically-inspired curriculum for instruction tuning. In SRS, reviews of learned material are scheduled at optimally increasing intervals to strengthen long-term memory. Adapted to LLM training, the curriculum would not just increase task complexity linearly but would algorithmically schedule the review and interleaving of previously mastered, simpler instruction types at strategic points during training on more complex tasks. This method aims to reinforce core skills, combat 'catastrophic forgetting' of foundational knowledge, and promote deeper integration of competencies, leading to models with more robust and generalizable instruction-following abilities.

ideas.title: Applying Immunological Tolerance Principles to Measure Instruction Tuning's Effect on Model Biases
ideas.description: This research adapts the immunological concept of 'immune tolerance' to study how large-scale instruction finetuning influences social biases and toxicity in model outputs. In immunology, tolerance is a tuned state preventing self-attack, tested by challenging the system with self-antigens. Analogously, we propose treating instruction tuning as a 'tolerance induction' process for the model. The study would involve creating a diagnostic suite of 'challenge prompts'—ambiguous or edge-case instructions designed to probe for latent biases—to systematically test where and how the model's 'tuned' behavior breaks down, moving beyond aggregate metrics to map the specific conditions and prompt characteristics that cause regressions or improvements in biased or toxic outputs.
